AI.motivation: Current AI-for-science work mostly scales a single reasoner (bigger models, longer context, single-user agents), but real scientific breakthroughs typically arise from teams with diverse expertise. The open problem is how to scale and coordinate these heterogeneous humans and AI systems so that insights discovered in one place reach the right people or tools elsewhere in the workflow. The authors want a principled way to network scientific reasoning rather than just enlarging individual agents.
AI.method: The authors design and implement Mycelium, an active shared workspace that connects multiple human researchers and AI agents. As users and agents work, Mycelium: (1) captures observations and hypotheses, (2) represents how they relate to an evolving team model of the problem, and (3) routes relevant information to the human or agent whose next decision could benefit from it. They deploy Mycelium in a real biological multi-omics study and analyze how routed context transforms isolated analytical findings into shared, mechanistic constraints and experimental designs. They also formalize the concept of networked intelligence as sparse conditional computation over distributed scientific contexts, providing a computational model of when networked setups are needed versus when a single scaled agent suffices.
AI.result: In the biological multi-omics case study, Mycelium successfully routes context so that a localized analytical insight is elevated into a cross-expert mechanistic constraint and then into a concrete experimental plan. This demonstrates that the system can turn fragmented, local reasoning into coordinated, multi-step scientific progress. The computational account clarifies conditions under which networked intelligence offers benefits beyond a single large agent, especially when expertise and contexts cannot simply be merged into one model.
AI.conclusion: Mycelium operationalizes networked intelligence for science by actively mediating information flow between humans and AI agents, going beyond standard single-agent AI-for-science approaches. The empirical deployment shows that structured routing of shared context can materially shape scientific reasoning and experiment design. Conceptually, modeling networked intelligence as sparse conditional computation over distributed contexts helps distinguish problems solvable by a powerful standalone agent from those that fundamentally require a coordinated network of diverse experts and tools.

title: A POS Tier Is the Key to Automated Annotation for Low-Resource Language Documentation: Neural Interlinear Glossing for Irabu, a Southern Ryukyuan Language
comment: 15 pages, 9 figures, 8 tables. Code, corpus, and complete per-sentence test outputs: https://github.com/MichinoriShimoji/ML_autogloss
summary: Discourse data are the primary empirical basis of grammar writing in field linguistics, but producing interlinearized text is notoriously expensive - on the order of one hour of work per minute of recording. For endangered languages, where the time remaining to verify analyses with native speakers is itself limited, automating parts of the interlinearization workflow has direct documentary value. We implement a full neural annotation pipeline (morpheme segmentation, POS tagging, glossing) for Irabu Ryukyuan using deliberately small, transparent BiLSTM-CRF models, and evaluate it under a realistic hard constraint: approximately one hour of fully annotated discourse as the entire supervised resource. Two factors of the annotation itself are manipulated: its richness (with or without a POS tier) and its quantity (training budgets from 6 to 47 minutes). Gold POS improves grammatical glossing by +4.4 (SD 0.7) points (significant in all 5 seeds), and the gain grows as data shrink (+11.6 points at a quarter of the data); a POS tier more than halves the amount of glossed data needed to reach a given accuracy. In a fully automatic pipeline this gain is not yet realized: the tagger still errs on 12% of morphemes, and an incorrect POS misleads the glossing model more than no POS at all. The value is latent rather than lost: degrading gold POS with controlled noise shows the gain returning as tagger accuracy rises, with break-even near our tagger's current 88% and +1.6 to +3.2 points recovered at 92-96%. We conclude with a concrete recommendation for documentation practice: annotate quadrilinearly - text, POS, gloss, translation.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a neural pipeline to help automate interlinear glossed text (morpheme segmentation, POS tagging, glossing) for a low-resource endangered language, and analyzes how much and what kind of annotation is most valuable under extreme data scarcity.
AI.motivation: Interlinear glossed texts are crucial for describing grammars in field linguistics, but they are very time-consuming to produce, especially for endangered languages where both speaker time and annotator time are limited. There is a need for practical automation that works with very little annotated data and informs how linguists should best spend their annotation effort (e.g., whether adding part-of-speech tiers pays off).
AI.method: They build a complete neural annotation pipeline for Irabu Ryukyuan using small, transparent BiLSTM-CRF models for three tasks: morpheme segmentation, POS tagging, and morphological glossing. They simulate realistic low-resource conditions, limiting fully annotated discourse to roughly one hour, and systematically vary (i) annotation richness (with vs. without POS tier) and (ii) annotation quantity (training budgets from 6 to 47 minutes of annotated speech). They then measure downstream glossing accuracy and run controlled experiments adding noise to gold POS to examine how POS tagger accuracy affects glossing quality.
AI.result: Gold POS tags improve grammatical glossing accuracy by about +4.4 points on average (SD 0.7), significant across 5 random seeds, with the benefit becoming even larger as the amount of training data shrinks (up to +11.6 points at a quarter of the data). Having a POS tier more than halves the amount of glossed data needed to reach a target accuracy. However, in a fully automatic pipeline, this potential gain is not realized because the POS tagger has about 12% error; incorrect POS tags hurt glossing more than omitting POS. Noise-injection experiments show that as POS tagging accuracy increases beyond ~88%, the benefits re-emerge, with +1.6 to +3.2 points in glossing accuracy recovered at 92–96% POS accuracy.
AI.conclusion: A POS annotation tier has substantial latent value for improving automatic glossing in low-resource, endangered language documentation, especially when data are scarce, but this value only materializes when POS tagging accuracy is sufficiently high. Under realistic annotation and modeling constraints, the authors recommend that field linguists use quadrilinear annotation (text, POS, gloss, translation) because POS information significantly improves the efficiency of creating high-quality interlinearized texts and reduces the total amount of data needed.

title: AI-Native Insurance for Agentic AI: Pricing, Underwriting, and End-to-End Automation
summary: Agentic AI introduces new insurance challenges because autonomous AI systems can make decisions, invoke tools, modify external environments, and interact with third-party services. This paper develops an AI-native mathematical framework for underwriting, pricing, and contract design for agentic AI deployments. A deployment is represented by a risk state that captures autonomy level, operational authority, permission exposure, governance maturity, and dependency concentration. The framework maps the risk state to event probabilities, loss severities, governance costs, premiums, deductibles, coverage allocation, and policy covenants, and formulates an optimization problem for insurance contract design under participation, profitability, and incentive compatibility constraints. The paper establishes structural properties of insurability, including characterization of an insurability region, monotone deterioration of feasibility with increasing exposure, and governance certification thresholds. Insurance is further interpreted as both an operational cost and a regulatory mechanism for AI deployment. A healthcare case study illustrates contract optimization, sensitivity analysis, and automated claims processing for agentic AI systems.

AI.tldr: Develops a mathematical insurance framework tailored to agentic AI deployments, characterizing risk, pricing, and contract design, and showing when and how such AI is insurable.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems act autonomously, invoke tools, and interact with external services, creating novel, complex, and hard-to-price risks that traditional insurance models are not designed to handle. Insurers and regulators need a principled way to evaluate and price these risks, determine when coverage is feasible, and use insurance as a governance tool for safe AI deployment.
AI.method: Define a formal risk-state representation for AI deployments based on autonomy, authority, permission exposure, governance maturity, and dependency concentration. Map this risk state to stochastic loss events and costs, and formulate an optimization problem for insurance contract design (premiums, deductibles, coverage allocation, covenants) under constraints such as participation, profitability, and incentive compatibility. Analyze the resulting optimization and risk-state space to derive structural properties and thresholds of insurability.
AI.result: The framework yields an insurability region over the space of AI risk states, shows that the feasibility of offering insurance deteriorates monotonically as exposure increases, and identifies governance certification thresholds beyond which insurance becomes viable. It produces conditions and formulas for event probabilities, loss severities, governance costs, and optimal pricing/contract terms. A healthcare case study demonstrates practical application for contract optimization, sensitivity analysis, and automated claims processing.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI requires AI-native insurance models; the proposed mathematical framework characterizes when and how such systems can be insured and how contract terms should adjust to their risk profile and governance maturity. Insurance emerges not only as a way to transfer and price risk but also as an operational cost and a regulatory lever that can shape safer AI deployment, as illustrated in a healthcare use case.

title: GFlowRL: Scaling Distribution-Matching RL to Large Language Models
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) offer a promising alternative to reward-maximizing reinforcement learning (RL) for large reasoning models, encouraging diverse reasoning paths by matching reward distributions rather than collapsing to dominant modes. Recent work shows promise on math and code, but scaling GFlowNet-style RL to modern post-training pipelines remains difficult: as model size, rollout horizon, reward noise, and distributed-systems complexity grow together, a learned prompt-conditional partition function becomes a source of gradient instability and engineering overhead rather than a useful normalizer. Through systematic analysis, we find that the learned partition function, previously treated as essential, can be replaced by an in-batch Monte Carlo estimate computed from the rollout group already required for training. We propose GFlowRL, a streamlined GFlowNet-style RL algorithm that removes the auxiliary partition network entirely while preserving the reward-distribution-matching objective, completed by two stabilizers: importance-sampling correction for rollout/trainer drift and asymmetric flow-gap clipping for outlier residuals. GFlowRL exceeds all counterparts on math, code, and adversarial red-teaming benchmarks, reaching a Codeforces rating of 2048 at the 14B scale (within 25 Elo of o3-mini) and attaining the highest average ASR@1 on AdvBench and HarmBench, outperforming the previous SOTA multi-turn attacker in a regime where FlowRL, a prior GFlowNet-style method, diverges. The same recipe transfers to all evaluated MoE configurations up to 235B parameters, where FlowRL again fails to converge. To our knowledge, GFlowRL is the first GFlowNet-style RL algorithm to scale stably across both dense and sparse architectures. Code will be at: https://github.com/microsoft/gflowrl

AI.tldr: They introduce GFlowRL, a simplified GFlowNet-style reinforcement learning algorithm that removes the learned partition function and instead uses an in-batch Monte Carlo estimate plus two stabilizers, achieving more stable and scalable training and state-of-the-art results on math, code, and red-teaming benchmarks up to 235B parameters.
AI.motivation: Standard reward-maximizing RL for reasoning models tends to collapse to a few dominant reasoning paths and struggles with diversity. GFlowNets help by matching the full reward distribution but, when scaled to large models and long rollouts, the learned partition function becomes unstable and hard to engineer. The authors want a GFlowNet-style method that scales cleanly in modern, large-scale post-training pipelines without this bottleneck.
AI.method: They systematically analyze the role of the learned prompt-conditional partition function in prior GFlowNet-style RL and show it can be replaced with an in-batch Monte Carlo estimate computed over the rollout group already used for training. Building on this, they design GFlowRL, which preserves the reward-distribution-matching objective but removes the auxiliary partition network. To maintain stability, they add (1) an importance-sampling correction to account for drift between the rollout policy and the training policy, and (2) asymmetric flow-gap clipping to limit the impact of outlier residuals. They then apply this algorithm to both dense and MoE LLMs at various scales.
AI.result: GFlowRL outperforms prior RL and GFlowNet-style baselines on math, coding, and adversarial red-teaming tasks. It achieves a Codeforces rating of 2048 at 14B parameters, within about 25 Elo of o3-mini. On AdvBench and HarmBench, it attains the highest average ASR@1, beating the previous state-of-the-art multi-turn attack model, while a prior GFlowNet-style method (FlowRL) diverges in this setting. The same training recipe successfully scales to MoE models up to 235B parameters, where FlowRL again fails to converge.
AI.conclusion: A learned partition function is not necessary for practical, large-scale GFlowNet-style RL. By replacing it with an in-batch Monte Carlo estimate and adding importance-sampling and asymmetric clipping stabilizers, GFlowRL becomes the first GFlowNet-style RL algorithm to scale stably across both dense and sparse LLM architectures, delivering state-of-the-art performance on challenging reasoning and red-teaming benchmarks. Code will be released for reproducibility and further research.

title: Cost-Optimal Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio for Transportation Management
summary: Foundation models, including large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), are increasingly used for transportation management center (TMC) tasks such as anomaly detection, incident reporting, and traveler information. Deploying multiple such models across TMC functions raises a portfolio question: which model should serve each function, in which deployment mode, and under what shared hardware budget? We formulate this as the Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio (FMDP) problem, a mixed-integer program minimizing total cost of ownership (TCO) subject to per-function quality, latency, and safety constraints over shared GPU capacity. We prove the problem NP-hard by reduction from the 0-1 knapsack problem and propose a polynomial-time greedy heuristic. In an illustrative case study with five TMC functions and 19 candidate (model, mode) pairs, FMDP identifies a mixed portfolio costing $34/mo (97% below the cheapest feasible all-closed-API baseline) by routing four functions to open-source APIs and the one function whose quality floor no open-source model meets to a closed API. Break-even analysis shows that on-premise GPU investment becomes reasonable only above approximately 309 vision queries/hour or if API prices double.

AI.tldr: The paper formulates and solves the Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio (FMDP) problem for transportation management centers, choosing which foundation models and deployment modes to use for each function under cost, quality, latency, safety, and shared GPU constraints.
AI.motivation: Transportation management centers increasingly rely on foundation models (LLMs and VLMs) for various tasks, but selecting and deploying multiple models across functions under a shared hardware budget is complex. There is a need for a systematic way to minimize total cost while satisfying performance and safety requirements.
AI.method: The authors formulate the Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio (FMDP) problem as a mixed-integer programming optimization that minimizes total cost of ownership (TCO) while enforcing per-function constraints on quality, latency, and safety over shared GPU capacity. They prove NP-hardness via reduction from the 0-1 knapsack problem and then design a polynomial-time greedy heuristic to obtain approximate solutions. They validate the approach using an illustrative case study with five TMC functions and 19 candidate (model, mode) pairs (including open-source and closed APIs, and on-premise options).
AI.result: In the case study, the FMDP framework selects a mixed deployment portfolio costing $34 per month, which is 97% cheaper than the least expensive feasible portfolio using only closed APIs. Four functions are assigned to open-source APIs, while one function that cannot meet its quality requirement with any open-source model is assigned to a closed API. Break-even analysis indicates that investing in on-premise GPUs only becomes cost-effective when vision query volume exceeds roughly 309 per hour or when API prices double.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing foundation model deployment across TMC functions as a portfolio problem can substantially reduce costs while meeting quality, latency, and safety requirements. The FMDP formulation and heuristic offer a practical decision-support tool, and the analysis suggests that cloud/API-based solutions are generally preferable at moderate workloads, with on-premise GPUs becoming attractive only beyond specific volume or price thresholds.

title: Demystifying On-Policy Distillation: Roles, Pathologies, and Regulations
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) has become a key paradigm in LLM post-training, yet its training dynamics remain poorly understood. We present a systematic study examining the role, pathologies, and regulations of OPD. We first clarify the role of OPD as an exploration catalyst: it steers the student toward correct reasoning paths via dense token-level guidance, without expanding capability ceiling. We confirm this by showing that prompt diversity matters more than per-problem sampling numbers, and critically, that the effectiveness of OPD hinges entirely on the quality of its guiding signal. This dependency exposes two pathologies that derail exploration. The Student-Teacher Mismatch occurs when a large teacher-student distributional gap causes the guiding signal to misalign with task correctness, steering exploration in counterproductive directions. Length Exploitation arises when the aggregated token-level objective creates length-dependent shortcuts, allowing the student to game the reward landscape through response truncation or redundant padding, exploring degenerate length modes rather than reasoning strategies. To tame these pathologies, we investigate lightweight signal regulations: advantage clipping and log-scale compression, ensuring exploration is guided by faithful signals. Experiments across seven benchmarks demonstrate that these regulations alleviate length exploitation and enable effective distillation, stably surpassing OPD variants and RLVR baselines, thereby confirming that well-regulated signal quality, rather than mere teacher scale, governs successful exploration in OPD.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how on-policy distillation (OPD) actually works for LLM post-training, identifies two key failure modes in its training dynamics, and proposes simple signal-regularization tricks that fix them and improve performance over existing OPD and RLVR methods.
AI.motivation: Although OPD is widely used to train and align large language models, people do not really understand why it works, under what conditions it fails, and what aspects (teacher size, data sampling, signal shaping) truly matter. Without this understanding, practitioners may over-invest in teacher scale or sampling while ignoring subtle signal pathologies that can derail exploration and limit performance. The paper aims to provide a systematic, mechanistic view of OPD’s role as an exploration mechanism, and to uncover and control the core issues that prevent OPD from reliably improving models.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical and conceptual study of OPD. They reinterpret OPD as an exploration catalyst that guides a student model via dense token-level teacher signals. They test hypotheses about what matters for OPD (e.g., prompt diversity vs. number of samples per prompt, teacher quality) and examine how the student’s training dynamics change under different signal conditions. They identify two specific failure modes—Student-Teacher Mismatch and Length Exploitation—by analyzing the alignment between teacher signals, task correctness, and length-sensitive reward aggregation. To mitigate these, they design and evaluate two simple signal-regularization techniques: (1) advantage clipping to limit extreme signal values and (2) log-scale compression to smooth the reward landscape and reduce length-based gaming. They evaluate these strategies across seven benchmarks and compare against existing OPD variants and RLVR baselines.
AI.result: The study finds that OPD does not increase the ultimate capability ceiling of the model but is valuable as an exploration aid, helping the student reach good reasoning paths more reliably. Prompt diversity is more beneficial than drawing many samples per problem, emphasizing exploration breadth. The effectiveness of OPD is shown to depend heavily on the quality and faithfulness of the teacher-guidance signal, not just on the teacher’s scale. Two key pathologies are documented: (1) Student-Teacher Mismatch, where distributional gaps lead the teacher’s signal to diverge from true task correctness, and (2) Length Exploitation, where token-level aggregation encourages the model to exploit response length (via truncation or padding) instead of learning better reasoning trajectories. Implementing advantage clipping and log-scale compression alleviates these issues, prevents degenerate length behaviors, and yields stable performance improvements over standard OPD and RLVR across seven benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: OPD primarily serves as an exploration-guiding mechanism, not a way to raise a model’s inherent capability ceiling. Its success is governed by the quality and regulation of the guiding signal rather than teacher size or naive sampling strategies. Unregulated OPD is vulnerable to student-teacher mismatch and length-based reward exploitation, which can misdirect exploration and harm learning. Simple, lightweight signal-regularization methods—advantage clipping and log-scale compression—substantially improve OPD by ensuring that exploration is driven by faithful, stable signals. With these regulations, OPD can reliably outperform common OPD variants and RLVR baselines, highlighting that careful signal design is central to effective post-training of LLMs.

title: Harness Handbook: Making Evolving Agent Harnesses Readable,Navigable, and Editable
summary: The capability of a modern AI agent depends not only on its foundation model but also on its harness, which constructs prompts, manages state, invokes tools, and coordinates execution. As models, APIs, environments, and requirements evolve, the harness must be continually modified. Before such a change can be made, a developer or coding agent must identify all code locations that implement the target behavior. This is difficult because production harnesses are large, tightly coupled, and behaviorally distributed, while modification requests describe what the system should do and repositories are organized by files and modules. Code search, repository indexing, and long-context processing ease inspection, but still leave this behavior-to-code mapping to be recovered by hand. Behavior localization is therefore a central bottleneck in harness evolution. We introduce the Harness Handbook, a behavior-centric representation synthesized automatically from a harness codebase via static analysis and LLM-assisted structuring, linking each behavior to its corresponding source. We also introduce Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD), which guides agents from high-level behaviors to relevant implementation details and verifies candidate locations against the current source. On diverse modification requests from two open-source harnesses, Handbook-Assisted planning improves behavior localization and edit-plan quality while using fewer planner tokens, with the largest gains on scattered sites, rarely executed paths, and cross-module interactions. Evolving complex agentic systems thus depends not only on generating edits, but also on determining where those edits should be made.

AI.tldr: They propose Harness Handbook, an automatically generated behavior-centered map from behaviors to code locations in AI agent harnesses, and Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD) to help localize behaviors and plan edits, improving modification of complex AI harnesses.
AI.motivation: Modern AI agents rely on complex harnesses around foundation models, and evolving these harnesses is hard because developers must manually find all code implementing a requested behavior. Existing tools help search but still require humans to recover the mapping from desired behavior to scattered code. This behavior localization problem slows down harness evolution.
AI.method: They statically analyze the harness codebase and use LLMs to synthesize a "Harness Handbook" that organizes the system around behaviors and links each described behavior to exact source locations. They introduce Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD), a procedure/agent workflow that starting from a high-level behavior request, navigates the Handbook, progressively reveals more detailed implementation info, and checks proposed edit locations against the current source. They evaluate this on modification tasks for two open-source harnesses.
AI.result: Using the Harness Handbook within BGPD improves agents' ability to localize relevant code and produce higher-quality edit plans for modification requests, while consuming fewer planner tokens. Gains are especially pronounced for behaviors implemented across scattered code sites, infrequently used paths, and cross-module interactions.
AI.conclusion: Effective evolution of complex AI agent harnesses requires not just strong code-editing agents but also explicit, behavior-centric maps from behaviors to code. Automatically synthesized Harness Handbooks and BGPD substantially reduce the central bottleneck of behavior localization, making harness modification more efficient and reliable.

title: Exploring Post-Training Alignment of Small Language Models for Biomedical Data-to-Text Generation: A Case Study of Medication Leaflet
summary: Translating complex biomedical data into patient-friendly narratives is central to modern biomedical informatics. This study presents a comparative analysis of training small language models (SLMs) in specialized biomedical datato-text generation tasks. We explore widely adopted post-training methods including supervised fine-tuning (SFT), direct preference optimization (DPO), odds ratio preference optimization (ORPO), and group relative policy optimization (GRPO) with Qwen-based SLMs on a medicine package leaflets dataset. To assess cross-dataset generalizability, we also curated drug label data from openFDA. We evaluate models using both standard lexical overlap metrics like ROUGE as well as semantic similarity measures. Across our experiments, the results show that (1) the aligned SLMs outperform proprietary models like GPT-5; (2) ORPO outperforms the SFTbaselines; (3) GRPO yields the most robust cross-dataset performance among the alignment methods tested as well as GPT-5.

AI.tldr: The paper compares different post-training methods for small language models on biomedical data-to-text generation, showing aligned small models can beat a large proprietary model and that GRPO generalizes best across datasets.
AI.motivation: Patients and clinicians need understandable narratives derived from complex biomedical and regulatory data. Existing large proprietary models may be strong but are costly and less controllable, and it is unclear which alignment/post-training techniques work best for small language models in specialized biomedical data-to-text tasks or how well they generalize across datasets.
AI.method: The authors train Qwen-based small language models on a medicine package leaflet data-to-text generation task using several post-training methods: supervised fine-tuning (SFT), direct preference optimization (DPO), odds ratio preference optimization (ORPO), and group relative policy optimization (GRPO). They further test cross-dataset generalization using curated drug label data from openFDA. Performance is evaluated with lexical overlap metrics (e.g., ROUGE) and semantic similarity measures, and compared against a proprietary GPT-5 baseline.
AI.result: Aligned Qwen-based small language models outperform a proprietary GPT-5 baseline on the biomedical data-to-text tasks. ORPO yields better performance than SFT baselines on the primary dataset, and GRPO provides the strongest and most stable performance across both the original medicine leaflet dataset and the external openFDA drug label dataset, also surpassing GPT-5 in cross-dataset settings.
AI.conclusion: Specialized alignment of small language models using advanced preference-based post-training can surpass large proprietary models for biomedical data-to-text generation. Among the methods studied, ORPO improves over standard SFT, and GRPO offers the best robustness and cross-dataset generalization, making it a promising strategy for deploying small, domain-specialized language models in biomedical informatics applications.

title: Theory-Level Autoformalization: From Isolated Statements to Unified Formal Knowledge Bases
summary: Autoformalization translates informal natural language into formal, machine-verifiable languages. While most work focuses on individual statements, real formalization efforts are inherently theory-level: they require an entire web of axioms, definitions, and lemmas before target theorems can even be stated. In this position paper, we argue for theory-level autoformalization: formalizing complete theories, including all their inter-dependencies, as structured libraries. We examine the significance of this shift, address alternative views, identify open challenges, and propose three promising paths forward. Our survey of autoformalization is available at https://github.com/marcusm117/Awesome-Autoformalization.

AI.tldr: The paper advocates shifting autoformalization from individual statements to whole theories and outlines motivations, challenges, and future directions for building complete formal libraries.
AI.motivation: Existing autoformalization largely treats each statement in isolation, but real-world formalization requires a network of related axioms, definitions, and lemmas. The authors want to highlight that focusing only on isolated statements misses the structure and dependencies that make up actual mathematical theories and formal libraries.
AI.method: This is a position/survey-style paper. The authors conceptually analyze current autoformalization work, argue for a theory-level perspective, discuss alternative viewpoints, catalog open problems, and suggest three strategic research directions. They also provide a curated survey resource via a GitHub repository.
AI.result: The main results are conceptual rather than empirical: a clarified framing of theory-level autoformalization, arguments for why it matters, a discussion of competing perspectives, identification of challenges, and three proposed research paths. They also provide a public survey of autoformalization work hosted on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Autoformalization research should move beyond single statements toward constructing full formal theories as structured libraries, including their interdependencies. Addressing the outlined challenges and following the suggested research directions is crucial for making automated formalization practically useful at scale.

title: When Rubrics Change: Cross-Rubric Generalization for Critical Thinking Essay Scoring
comment: Published in AI for Education Day at SIGKDD 2026
summary: Automated essay scoring (AES) research has largely focused on cross-prompt generalization, where essays from unseen prompts are scored while the scoring criteria are typically held constant. In practice, however, educators may revise or even introduce new rubrics in their scoring task, to evaluate different aspects of essays. We study cross-rubric generalization: training on essays labeled under one set of rubrics and evaluating on previously unseen rubrics, which target different aspects of the essay. We use a Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning framework with two components: rubric-agnostic intermediate representations, called traits, and target-essay supervision under seen rubrics during training. On an AES dataset augmented with multiple rubric-defined labels of student critical thinking skills, we find that traits improve macro F1 by 5.0% over a baseline without traits in the hardest setting, where both target rubrics and target essays are unseen during training. We further find that increasing target-essay supervision improves performance, with our best fine-tuned open-source Llama-based model outperforming GPT-5-mini prompting by 2.1% macro F1 and trailing GPT-5 by 1.9%. These results show that trait-based intermediate structure and controlled supervision improve generalization to unseen rubrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and evaluates a method for automated essay scoring that can generalize to new, previously unseen scoring rubrics, not just new prompts.
AI.motivation: Most automated essay scoring systems assume the scoring rubric—what aspects of writing are evaluated and how—is fixed. In real educational settings, teachers often modify or replace rubrics to assess different dimensions such as critical thinking or argument quality. Existing work mainly focuses on cross-prompt generalization (new essay prompts, same rubric), leaving a gap in handling cross-rubric generalization (new scoring criteria). The paper aims to close this gap so that models remain useful when rubrics change.
AI.method: The authors formulate a cross-rubric generalization setting: train models on essays labeled under one set of rubrics and evaluate them on essays labeled with different, unseen rubrics. They propose an LLM fine-tuning framework with two key elements: (1) learning rubric-agnostic intermediate representations called “traits,” which capture general essay properties relevant across rubrics; and (2) using target-essay supervision under seen rubrics during training, i.e., directly training on target-style essay labels where available. They implement this framework using open-source Llama-based models and compare against prompting GPT-style models, measuring macro F1 on a dataset of student essays annotated with multiple rubric-defined critical thinking labels.
AI.result: On an AES dataset annotated with multiple rubric-based labels of students’ critical thinking skills, incorporating trait-based intermediate representations yields a 5.0% macro F1 improvement over a baseline without traits in the most difficult condition, where both the target rubrics and the target essays are unseen during training. Additionally, performance improves as the amount of target-essay supervision is increased. The best fine-tuned open-source Llama-based model surpasses a GPT-5-mini prompting baseline by 2.1% macro F1 and is only 1.9% macro F1 behind a stronger GPT-5 model.
AI.conclusion: Trait-based intermediate structure and carefully controlled target-essay supervision significantly enhance an AES model’s ability to generalize across rubrics, not just across prompts. This suggests that designing rubric-agnostic internal representations and leveraging limited supervision on target-style essays are effective strategies for making essay scoring systems robust to changing or newly introduced rubrics in educational practice.

title: EZSMT Version 3, Matured
comment: Under consideration in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)
summary: Constraint Answer Set Programming (CASP) is a hybrid reasoning paradigm that combines Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Constraint Processing and Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT), enabling powerful declarative encodings of complex combinatorial search problems. This paper presents the design and implementation of EZSMTV3, an extensible SMT-based CASP framework that advances the translational approach to CASP solving. Building upon the foundation of the EZSMT+ system, EZSMTV3 introduces a more expressive input language, supports optimization via weak constraints, and offers foundations for streamlined integration of new constraint types. Rather than implementing custom search procedures, EZSMTV3 leverages state-of-the-art SMT solvers, such as CVC5, YICES, and Z3 to perform reasoning. The paper provides benchmarking results comparing EZSMTV3 with its CASP peers such as CLINGCON, CLINGO[DL], and CLINGO[LP], while showcasing its ability to handle mixed-domain constraints involving both integers and reals. The system provides a robust platform for future extensions and theoretical exploration within the CASP domain.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EZSMTV3, an extensible SMT-based framework for Constraint Answer Set Programming that supports a richer language, optimization, and easier integration of new constraints, while delegating search to modern SMT solvers and performing competitively with existing CASP systems.
AI.motivation: Existing CASP systems either rely on specialized, hard-to-extend search procedures or have limited expressiveness and support for optimization and mixed-domain constraints. There is a need for a flexible, extensible CASP framework that can encode complex combinatorial problems, easily incorporate new constraint types, and exploit advances in SMT solvers without re-implementing search algorithms.
AI.method: The authors adopt a translational approach to CASP: high-level CASP programs are translated into SMT instances that can be handled by off-the-shelf SMT solvers like CVC5, YICES, and Z3. They design a more expressive input language, add support for weak constraints for optimization, and architect EZSMTV3 so that new constraint domains can be integrated in a modular fashion. They then empirically evaluate the system against other CASP tools (CLINGCON, CLINGO[DL], CLINGO[LP]) on benchmarks, including problems with both integer and real constraints.
AI.result: EZSMTV3 successfully supports a richer CASP language, including weak constraints for optimization and mixed integer-real constraints, and can be connected to multiple state-of-the-art SMT solvers. Benchmarking shows that its performance is competitive with established CASP systems and that the translational SMT-based approach scales well across various problem domains, particularly in mixed-domain scenarios.
AI.conclusion: EZSMTV3 demonstrates that an extensible, SMT-based translational approach to CASP can provide both expressiveness and practical performance without custom search procedures. The system forms a robust basis for adding new constraint types, exploring optimization in CASP, and conducting further theoretical and practical research on integrating ASP with SMT and constraint processing.

title: Live Gurbani Tracking: A Benchmark and Reference System for Captioning Sikh Kirtan
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables. Benchmark, scorer, and reference system released. Live deployment and on-device iOS app available; see paper for links
summary: We present a benchmark and reference system for live captioning of Sikh Kirtan - the continuous, sung recitation of verses from the Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji (SGGS). Unlike open-vocabulary lyrics transcription, Kirtan captioning is a closed-vocabulary problem: every displayed line must be an exact, word-for-word line from the canonical scripture, because displaying misspelled Gurmukhi is considered religiously inappropriate. We formalize the task as predicting, at every time t, a pair (shabad_id, line_idx) or null, and organize the problem space into a 2x2 matrix along two orthogonal axes: live vs. offline (causal vs. full-audio access) and blind vs. oracle (shabad identity discovered vs. given). We release v1 of the benchmark - 4 hand-annotated Kirtan recordings x 3 cold-start offsets = 12 evaluation cases, ~57 minutes of scored audio - together with a scorer that computes frame accuracy at 1s resolution over a scored region, with a 1s collar and gap-tolerant scoring at segment boundaries. We describe a reference system (fine-tuned 120M IndicConformer -> fuzzy matcher -> state machine; INT8 ONNX; RTF ~0.05 on one Apple Silicon core) that achieves 57.9% overall frame accuracy across all 12 cases (10/12 correct shabad locks) on the hardest variant (live x blind). We compare against three trivial baselines (empty, shifted-5s, perfect) and discuss why standard ASR metrics (WER/CER) measure transcription accuracy rather than the display accuracy this task requires. The benchmark, reference system, and a live deployment are released under permissive licenses to facilitate further improvements.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark and reference system for live, closed-vocabulary captioning of Sikh Kirtan verses from the Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji, framing it as a structured detection task rather than generic ASR and releasing data, metrics, and a baseline system.
AI.motivation: Standard ASR and lyrics transcription work in open-vocabulary settings and are evaluated with WER/CER, which focus on transcription accuracy. For Sikh Kirtan, every displayed line must match exactly a verse from the canonical scripture, where misspellings are not acceptable. This creates a closed-vocabulary, display-accuracy–critical problem not well captured by existing benchmarks or metrics. The authors are motivated to: (1) formalize this unique task, (2) provide a realistic evaluation benchmark with appropriate metrics, and (3) supply a working reference system to catalyze research and practical deployments for live Kirtan captioning.
AI.method: They formalize Kirtan captioning as, at each time t, predicting either a null label or a pair (shabad_id, line_idx), representing the current verse and line index from the scripture. They structure the problem space in a 2x2 matrix along two axes: live vs. offline (causal vs. full-audio) and blind vs. oracle (whether the shabad identity must be discovered or is given a priori). For evaluation, they create a v1 benchmark consisting of four hand-annotated Kirtan recordings, each evaluated from three different cold-start offsets, yielding 12 evaluation cases (~57 minutes). They design a scorer that operates at 1s frame resolution with a 1s collar and gap-tolerant scoring at segment boundaries, computing frame accuracy over a scored region. As a reference system, they fine-tune a 120M-parameter IndicConformer ASR model, then post-process its outputs through a fuzzy matcher and a state machine to map audio to canonical scripture lines. The system is quantized to INT8, exported as ONNX, and runs in real time with RTF ~0.05 on an Apple Silicon core. They also implement three trivial baselines: an empty output, a uniformly shifted output by 5 seconds, and a perfect oracle baseline.
AI.result: On the most challenging setting (live x blind, i.e., causal processing with unknown shabad identity), the reference system achieves an overall frame accuracy of 57.9% across all 12 evaluation cases and correctly locks onto the shabad in 10 out of 12 cases. The trivial baselines perform significantly worse, demonstrating that the task is non-trivial yet solvable to a meaningful degree. Standard ASR metrics such as WER and CER are shown to be poorly aligned with the requirements of this task, as they measure transcription word/character errors rather than the correctness of displayed canonical lines over time. The benchmark and scorer run efficiently and support systematic comparisons across methods.
AI.conclusion: The authors provide the first focused benchmark, metric, and reference system for live captioning of Sikh Kirtan as a closed-vocabulary, scripture-aligned task. Their framing of the problem, coupled with the released data, scorer, and baseline model, establishes a foundation for future research on accurate, low-latency Kirtan captioning. They argue that display accuracy over time, not raw transcription accuracy, is the key measure of quality, and they show that a practical, efficient system can already achieve moderate performance on realistic audio. By releasing everything under permissive licenses and demonstrating a live deployment, they aim to encourage further community-driven improvements and broader adoption of such systems.

title: Set-shifting Behavioral Test for Harnessed Agents
summary: What happens to an LLM agent's tool choice when the reliable tool silently changes within an ongoing session? We borrow set-shifting from cognitive psychology to study how well agents adapt to hidden reliability shifts. Our benchmark mounts tool-skill libraries with redundancies, where many tools solve the same task but differ in hidden reliability. In our evaluation framework, a branched schedule shifts the reliable tool group at hidden boundaries and pairs every shift with a no-shift control. We find that agents, by default, settle on a small recurring routine within a few turns of each boundary, with call shares concentrating on a few discrete values after each reliability shift. We score the set-shifting accuracy for each agent trajectory: the joint probability of routing to the target tool group in every post-shift window. We test open-weight LLMs in an open-source agentic harness and find qualitatively distinct failure modes across the same set of routines. We also find that set framing, how the toolset presents the alternatives as competing or complementary, shifts the routing dynamics.

AI.tldr: Study of how LLM agents adapt their tool choices when the most reliable tool silently changes mid-session.
AI.motivation: LLM agents often use external tools. In realistic settings, tool reliability can change without notice, but we lack a principled way to measure how well agents adapt their routing strategies to such hidden changes. Borrowing the concept of set-shifting from cognitive psychology, the paper aims to characterize and benchmark this adaptive behavior.
AI.method: They build a benchmark with tool libraries that have redundant tools for the same task but differing in hidden reliability. Using a branched evaluation schedule, they introduce hidden boundaries where the most reliable tool group changes, along with corresponding no-shift controls. They log agent tool calls across turns, analyze how usage concentrates on specific routines after each boundary, and define a set-shifting accuracy metric as the joint probability of routing to the currently reliable tool group in all post-shift windows. They run open-weight LLMs in an open-source agent harness and compare behaviors and failures.
AI.result: Agents quickly converge to small, recurring tool-use routines a few turns after each reliability boundary, and tool-call frequencies concentrate on a small set of discrete patterns after each reliability shift. Different open-weight LLMs exhibit qualitatively different failure modes despite using similar routines. The way tools are framed in the interface, especially whether tools are presented as competing vs. complementary, significantly changes routing dynamics.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents show limited but structured adaptability to hidden shifts in tool reliability, often locking into stable routines that may or may not track the currently reliable tool. The proposed benchmark and set-shifting metric reveal distinct adaptation failure modes across models and highlight that interface-level design choices, such as how tools are framed, can materially influence tool routing behavior.

title: DevicesWorld: Benchmarking Cross-Device Agents in Heterogeneous Environments
summary: LLM-based agents have rapidly improved at operating individual digital environments such as mobile applications, desktop systems, and smart homes. However, real-world user goals often span multiple devices: information may come from a phone, be processed on a desktop, and the result may need to appear on another device. Most existing benchmarks center on a single dominant execution environment, making it difficult to evaluate whether agents can acquire and integrate information across heterogeneous devices and complete end-to-end tasks with cross-device dependencies. We introduce DevicesWorld, a large-scale executable benchmark for cross-device collaborative operation. DevicesWorld contains 6,140 tasks and integrates three classes of device environments -- mobile, desktop, and IoT -- into a unified cross-device interaction and evaluation framework. Each task defines a natural-language user goal, participating devices and initial states, executable actions, rule-based verifiers, and a cleanup procedure. A multi-stage construction and quality-control pipeline keeps tasks close to realistic user needs while allowing final outcomes to be automatically verified from device states and generated files. We evaluate five frontier LLM-agent systems on a fixed evaluation set. All methods achieve low success rates, with the best reaching only 12.5%. Among failed runs, about 28.7% satisfy at least one scoring condition yet still fail the full task. Trajectories show that agents become stuck acquiring information or manipulating interfaces, confuse source and output devices, or terminate before all conditions are jointly satisfied. DevicesWorld turns cross-device collaborative operation into an executable, reproducible, and diagnostically useful evaluation problem for research on reliable cross-device agents.

AI.tldr: DevicesWorld is a large-scale benchmark for testing how well LLM agents can complete realistic tasks that require coordinated operation across mobile, desktop, and IoT devices.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based agents are typically evaluated in single-device environments, while many real-world user goals require coordinated actions across multiple heterogeneous devices. There is a lack of executable, large-scale, and realistic benchmarks that can systematically test whether agents can acquire, integrate, and act on information across devices to satisfy complex, interdependent goals.
AI.method: The authors design DevicesWorld, an executable benchmark that integrates three classes of device environments—mobile, desktop, and IoT—into a unified interaction and evaluation framework. They construct 6,140 tasks, each with a natural-language goal, specified participating devices and initial states, allowed executable actions, rule-based verifiers for success conditions, and automated cleanup procedures. A multi-stage pipeline is used to generate tasks that resemble realistic user needs while ensuring that outcomes are automatically verifiable. They then evaluate five state-of-the-art LLM-based agent systems on a fixed subset of these tasks.
AI.result: Across the evaluated systems, success rates are low, with the best-performing agent achieving only 12.5% task completion. Analysis of failed trajectories shows that about 28.7% of failures partially satisfy some scoring conditions but still do not meet the full task requirements. The logs reveal common failure patterns such as agents getting stuck when acquiring information or manipulating interfaces, confusing source vs. output devices, and terminating early before all success conditions are jointly satisfied.
AI.conclusion: DevicesWorld provides an executable, reproducible, and diagnostic benchmark for cross-device collaborative operation, revealing that current LLM-based agents struggle significantly with realistic multi-device tasks. The benchmark can guide future research by exposing specific weaknesses—such as cross-device coordination, reliable state tracking, and interface manipulation—and by serving as a standard testbed for improving the robustness and reliability of cross-device agents.

title: LAPO: Leave-One-Turn Attribution for Self-Generated Process Rewards in Multi-Turn Search Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning for multi-turn search reasoning typically relies on terminal outcome rewards, which cannot distinguish useful, redundant, and harmful intermediate interactions. We propose LAPO, a self-generated process-supervision method based on backward leave-one-turn attribution. For each search turn, LAPO replaces the turn and its retrieval observation with a fixed [DELETE] placeholder and measures the resulting change in the current policy's mean log-likelihood of the gold answer. This Answer-Likelihood Gain estimates the turn's contribution while preserving all downstream interactions, allowing early evidence to be evaluated in the complete reasoning context. LAPO further applies sign-consistency gating, retaining only normalized process advantages whose directions agree with their raw attribution scores. The method requires no additional reward model, teacher, verifier, or LLM-as-a-Judge. Across seven knowledge-intensive question-answering datasets with local retrieval, LAPO achieves an average exact-match score of 0.326, outperforming the strongest step-reward baseline, IGPO, by 0.053. Ablations show complementary benefits from backward attribution and sign-consistency gating, demonstrating that policy-derived retrospective attribution can provide effective process supervision for multi-turn search agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LAPO, a self-generated process-supervision method to better train multi-turn search agents by attributing the contribution of each turn to the final answer likelihood.
AI.motivation: Existing reinforcement learning methods for multi-turn search reasoning use only terminal outcome rewards, which fail to differentiate between helpful, redundant, and harmful intermediate steps, limiting the effectiveness and robustness of training search agents.
AI.method: LAPO performs backward leave-one-turn attribution by replacing each search turn and its retrieval observation with a [DELETE] placeholder and measuring how much the current policy’s mean log-likelihood of the gold answer changes. This Answer-Likelihood Gain is used as a process reward. To improve robustness, LAPO uses sign-consistency gating, keeping only normalized process advantages whose sign (direction) is consistent with their raw attribution scores. The approach is fully self-generated and does not rely on external reward models, teachers, or judges.
AI.result: On seven knowledge-intensive QA datasets with local retrieval, LAPO attains an average exact-match score of 0.326, which is 0.053 higher than the best step-reward baseline, IGPO. Ablation studies verify that both backward attribution and sign-consistency gating contribute complementary gains.
AI.conclusion: Policy-derived retrospective attribution via LAPO yields effective process supervision for multi-turn search agents, enabling finer-grained evaluation of intermediate reasoning steps and leading to improved performance over existing step-reward baselines without extra supervision infrastructure.

title: MyAG: A Graph-Based Framework for Designing and Analyzing Composable LLM Agent Systems
summary: We present MyAG, a graph-based framework for designing and analyzing composable LLM agent systems. Our framework separates agent system construction into three graph abstractions: a component graph for agents, environments, and modules; a workflow graph for execution control; and a search graph for runtime execution. This separation allows users to flexibly reuse the same components with different strategies. We further support hierarchical composition through recursive system nodes and provide monitoring and visualization tools for inspecting agent execution. Experiments on representative agent applications show that our framework supports flexible agent system design and helps analyze performance-efficiency tradeoffs. Our framework is publicly available and fully open-source.

AI.tldr: MyAG is a graph-based, open-source framework for building, composing, and analyzing LLM agent systems via three complementary graph abstractions.
AI.motivation: LLM agent systems are complex to design, reuse, and analyze, especially when coordinating multiple agents, tools, and control flows. Existing approaches often entangle components with execution logic, making it hard to flexibly reconfigure systems or understand performance-efficiency tradeoffs. The paper aims to provide a structured, composable abstraction that separates concerns and enables systematic design and analysis of agent systems.
AI.method: The authors introduce MyAG, which models an LLM agent system with three distinct but related graphs: (1) a component graph that defines agents, environments, and modules as reusable building blocks; (2) a workflow graph that specifies execution control and coordination among components; and (3) a search graph that represents runtime execution and exploration during inference. The framework supports hierarchical composition via recursive system nodes and includes monitoring and visualization tools to inspect and debug agent behavior. They evaluate MyAG on representative agent applications.
AI.result: Using MyAG on several representative agent-based applications, the authors demonstrate that the framework can express diverse agent designs and strategies by reusing the same components in different workflows. The experiments show that the structured graph abstractions help practitioners explore and understand performance versus efficiency tradeoffs across alternative designs and execution strategies.
AI.conclusion: MyAG provides a flexible, graph-based abstraction for constructing, composing, and analyzing LLM agent systems. By cleanly separating components, control workflows, and runtime search, it enables reuse, hierarchical composition, and better introspection of agent behavior. The open-source framework can facilitate more systematic design, debugging, and optimization of complex LLM agent applications.

title: How Far Can Root Cause Analysis Go on Real-World Telemetry Data?
summary: Identifying root causes in production microservice failures requires reasoning over large-scale, multimodal telemetry spanning metrics, logs, and traces, a problem that has proved resistant to both classical and LLM-based approaches. The OpenRCA dataset exemplifies these challenges: it is large-scale, multimodal, and lacks detailed domain knowledge, and yields consistently low accuracy across all existing methods. We show that classical causal discovery methods and existing LLM-based multi-agent systems fail to reliably identify root causes on this benchmark, and present a Structured Multi-Agent RCA pipeline that substantially outperforms existing LLM-based and classical baselines, supporting both domain-knowledge and knowledge-free operating modes. To diagnose where failures originate, we introduce a reverse reasoning agent that, given the correct answer, identifies which signals in the extracted anomalies support it and determines whether Stage~1 had access to those signals, classifying each failure as Reasoning Gap (evidence present but unused) or Data Ambiguity (evidence genuinely absent). This analysis reveals that the required evidence is present in the vast majority of failures: the bottleneck is not data access but the agent's ability to reason over it correctly. We further introduce an automated rule mining pipeline that systematically extracts discrimination rules from reverse reasoning reports, reducing reliance on manual knowledge curation. Across all configurations, model reasoning capability and domain knowledge are the primary constraints: stronger models embed more domain expertise, and explicit knowledge injection partially compensates for this gap. Reasoning performance remains practically bounded even when evidence extraction is perfect: scaffold engineering and better data pipelines alone cannot close this gap; progress requires improvements at the model level.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a structured multi-agent system for root cause analysis (RCA) on complex microservice telemetry, shows it outperforms classical and prior LLM methods on the OpenRCA benchmark, and finds that reasoning limits in models—not lack of data—are the main bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Root cause analysis in large-scale microservice production environments requires integrating heterogeneous telemetry sources (metrics, logs, traces). Existing classical causal discovery and LLM-based systems perform poorly on challenging benchmarks like OpenRCA, where data is multimodal, large, and lacks rich domain annotations. There is a need to understand why existing methods fail, disentangle data limitations from reasoning limitations, and design an RCA pipeline that can effectively use available evidence with minimal manual domain knowledge.
AI.method: The authors build a Structured Multi-Agent RCA pipeline that operates in both knowledge-free and domain-knowledge-augmented modes. It uses stages for anomaly extraction and reasoning, and introduces a reverse reasoning agent: given the ground-truth root cause, this agent inspects extracted anomalies to find supportive signals and check if earlier stages had access to them. Each failure case is labeled either as a Reasoning Gap (evidence present but unused) or Data Ambiguity (evidence missing). They also develop an automated rule mining pipeline that processes reverse reasoning reports to derive discrimination rules, encoding domain knowledge and reducing manual rule authoring. They evaluate against classical causal discovery and existing LLM multi-agent baselines on the OpenRCA dataset under multiple configurations and model strengths.
AI.result: On the OpenRCA benchmark, the proposed structured multi-agent pipeline significantly outperforms both classical causal discovery methods and existing LLM-based multi-agent systems across metrics. The reverse reasoning analysis shows that for most failure cases the necessary evidential signals are actually present in the anomalies; failures mainly stem from the model’s inability to reason correctly over this evidence. The automated rule mining successfully extracts useful discrimination rules that improve performance and reduce the need for hand-crafted knowledge. Stronger underlying language models, which implicitly contain more domain knowledge, show better RCA performance, and explicit domain knowledge injection further improves results.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that in complex microservice RCA, the main limitation is not data availability but the reasoning capability and embedded domain knowledge of the models. Even with perfect evidence extraction and sophisticated scaffolding, reasoning performance plateaus, implying that better orchestration alone cannot solve RCA. Effective progress requires advances in base model reasoning and domain expertise, supplemented—but not replaced—by structured multi-agent pipelines and automated knowledge extraction tools.

title: Cost-Pragmatic Quality Gating and Selection-Fusion Multi-Model Combiners for BioASQ Phases A+ and B
summary: We describe our BioASQ Task 14B 2026 system. The work centers on two design decisions: how aggressively to re-retrieve when first-stage retrieval is weak, and how to combine multiple language-model answers. Retrieval unions two parallel pipelines - a hybrid first stage (dense BGE + BM25 + RRF, reaching R@200 = 99.3% on the BioASQ-13b historical archive) and an agent-driven pipeline that decomposes the question over PubMed, Europe PMC, and iCite - with a BGE cross-encoder quality gate flagging weakly-supported questions for selective re-retrieval. On Task 12B 2024 validation, a cost-pragmatic re-retrieval policy beats a skill-strict baseline significantly on list F1 and list precision, at 12% lower re-retrieval cost. Holding prompt and model fixed across val and test 13B (different question sets), list F1 rises by +0.132 absolute on the BioASQ-released gold-input pool, consistent with substantial retrieval-side headroom. For Phase B answering we decompose multi-model ensemble lift into a selection component bounded by the per-question oracle and a fusion component that aggregators can exceed. The decomposition predicts before any experiment that LLM-as-judge wins on selection-dominated metrics (yes/no, multi-reference ROUGE) but is structurally insufficient on the recall component of fusion-friendly metrics (factoid rank-1, list recall). On Task 13B 2025 our synonym-union resolver wins list recall on every head, while GPT-5.5 solo retains the list-F1 lead because the resolver's wider item set costs precision. On the Task 14B 2026 preliminary leaderboard our team places first on the combined-exact aggregate on three of the eight (phase x batch) leaderboards, wins four individual question-type cells, and takes #1 on Phase B b3 ideal.

AI.tldr: BioASQ Task 14B 2026 system focusing on smarter re-retrieval and multi-model answer combination, achieving strong leaderboard performance.
AI.motivation: Improve biomedical question answering in BioASQ by better handling weak initial retrieval and exploiting multiple LLM answers effectively, while controlling computational cost.
AI.method: Two-part approach: (1) Retrieval uses hybrid dense+BM25+RRF first-stage plus an agent-driven decomposition pipeline over PubMed/Europe PMC/iCite, gated by a BGE cross-encoder that triggers selective re-retrieval under a cost-pragmatic policy; (2) Answering uses multi-model ensembles analyzed as selection vs fusion, with LLM-as-judge for selection-heavy metrics and a synonym-union resolver to boost recall on fusion-friendly metrics.
AI.result: On historical and validation data, retrieval achieves high recall (R@200=99.3%) and the cost-pragmatic re-retrieval policy outperforms a stricter baseline on list F1 and precision with 12% lower cost; list F1 on test improves by +0.132 while holding prompts/models fixed, indicating retrieval headroom. For answering, synonym-union resolver wins list recall across heads but trades off precision, while GPT-5.5 solo keeps best list F1. The system ranks first on multiple BioASQ 14B 2026 preliminary leaderboards and metrics, including Phase B b3 ideal.
AI.conclusion: Targeted re-retrieval guided by a quality gate and a cost-aware policy can substantially improve biomedical QA performance without proportional cost increase, and decomposing ensemble gains into selection and fusion components clarifies when LLM-as-judge or fusion-based methods are preferable; these choices yield state-of-the-art BioASQ performance on several metrics.

title: Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning with Tool-Augmented Evidence for Urban Region Profiling
summary: Urban region profiling constitutes a core problem in urban computing, supporting applications such as population estimation, economic assessment, and environmental monitoring. Existing methods typically formulate this task as multimodal representation learning, fusing heterogeneous urban data, e.g., satellite imagery, points of interest, textual descriptions, and 3D building information, into latent embeddings for prediction. However, these approaches are largely correlation-driven, assume cross-modal consistency, and rely on static pipelines, which limit their robustness in heterogeneous or unseen urban regions. We propose UrbanAgent, an agentic framework that reframes urban region profiling as a reasoning-driven inference problem. UrbanAgent instantiates an independent agent for each data modality and performs structured multi-agent collaborative reasoning to explicitly address cross-modal inconsistencies rather than absorbing them into a single representation. In addition, UrbanAgent extends indicator prediction as a closed-loop process of active evidence acquisition and iterative reasoning, enabling agents to verify uncertain inferences through tool-augmented retrieval of external knowledge optimized via reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments on global urban datasets for Carbon emissions, GDP, and Population estimation show that UrbanAgent consistently outperforms existing baselines, achieving an average improvement of 8.1% in R2, and exhibiting strong generalization performance in unseen-city settings.

AI.tldr: Agent-based, reasoning-driven framework (UrbanAgent) for urban region profiling with multiple data modalities, improving robustness and prediction accuracy over correlation-based multimodal embeddings.
AI.motivation: Urban profiling tasks (population, GDP, carbon emissions) need to integrate heterogeneous urban data. Existing multimodal embedding approaches are correlation-driven, assume consistent signals across modalities, and use static pipelines, which makes them brittle in heterogeneous or unseen urban regions. There is a need for a more reasoning-centric, adaptive, and robust framework that can explicitly handle cross-modal inconsistencies and dynamically acquire evidence.
AI.method: UrbanAgent treats each data modality (satellite images, POIs, text, 3D building data, etc.) as an independent agent. These agents engage in structured multi-agent collaborative reasoning, instead of collapsing all modalities into a single latent embedding. The framework formulates profiling as a closed-loop process: agents iteratively reason about target indicators, identify uncertainty, and actively acquire additional evidence via tool-augmented retrieval of external knowledge. The retrieval behavior is optimized using reinforcement learning to improve verification and inference quality.
AI.result: On global urban datasets for estimating carbon emissions, GDP, and population, UrbanAgent outperforms prior multimodal baselines. It achieves an average improvement of 8.1% in R² and demonstrates strong generalization in unseen-city scenarios, indicating robustness to distribution shifts and heterogeneous conditions.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-driven, multi-agent approaches can provide more robust and generalizable urban region profiling than conventional correlation-based multimodal representation learning. By explicitly modeling cross-modal inconsistencies and closing the loop between inference and evidence acquisition, UrbanAgent achieves higher accuracy and better transfer to unseen cities, suggesting a promising direction for urban computing tasks that rely on heterogeneous data sources.

title: Graded Entity-Familiarity Readouts in Language Models: Polish Adaptation, Cross-Language Robustness, and Refusal Steering
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures, 5 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/agentGreg/bielik-entity-familiarity
summary: Can a language model estimate its familiarity with an entity before generating an answer? We study activations at the final prompt token in twelve instruction-tuned models from the Bielik, PLLuM, Gemma-4, and Qwen3 families, using a new dataset of 1,440 Polish entities spanning four domains and ten Wikipedia-pageview deciles, plus fabricated controls. Familiarity-probe scores separate real from fabricated entities in every family; in the Polish-adapted Bielik and PLLuM families they additionally track entity popularity (model-mean Spearman $ρ$ 0.28-0.57, versus at most 0.11 in Gemma-4 and Qwen3), a pattern more strongly associated with Polish adaptation than with parameter count in this model sample. In a paired experiment on two families, probes retain 96-101% of within-language AUROC when the Polish question stem is replaced with an English one around unchanged entity names, showing robustness to prompt language in this setting. In Gemma-4-12B, the only model that natively refuses, adding a one-dimensional familiarity direction at a single layer moves refusal rates monotonically in both directions (0.24 to 1.00 on well-known entities; 0.73 to 0.00 on unknown ones). Finally, a calibrated familiarity probe is competitive among pre-generation abstention gates, although post-generation detectors better predict behavioral error on average. These results support a graded pre-generation entity-familiarity readout, and a separation between representational familiarity and the policy that converts it into abstention.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether language models can estimate how familiar they are with specific entities before generating answers, using internal activations as a signal.
AI.motivation: Abstention and answer calibration are important for deploying language models safely. Models should ideally know when they "don’t know"—for example, when asked about obscure or nonexistent entities—and abstain or hedge. Current methods mostly rely on post-generation signals (like log probabilities or detectors). The authors want to know if there is a robust, graded pre-generation signal of entity familiarity in the model’s internal representations that could be used for better abstention and calibration, especially across languages like Polish.
AI.method: They construct a dataset of 1,440 Polish entities across four domains and ten Wikipedia pageview popularity deciles, plus fabricated (nonexistent) entities as controls. They analyze activations at the final prompt token in twelve instruction-tuned models from the Bielik, PLLuM, Gemma-4, and Qwen3 families. They train linear probes on these activations to estimate entity familiarity: separating real from fabricated entities and predicting popularity deciles. They also test language robustness by swapping Polish question stems for English ones while keeping entity names the same. For Gemma-4-12B, which has a native refusal behavior, they identify a one-dimensional familiarity direction at a specific layer and intervene along this direction to see how refusal rates change. Finally, they compare a calibrated familiarity probe as a pre-generation abstention gate against post-generation error detectors in predicting model errors.
AI.result: 1) Familiarity-probe scores can reliably distinguish real from fabricated entities across all four model families. 2) In Bielik and PLLuM (Polish-adapted) models, probe scores correlate moderately with entity popularity (Spearman ρ ≈ 0.28–0.57), whereas Gemma-4 and Qwen3 show far weaker correlations (≤ 0.11), suggesting adaptation to Polish matters more than scale in this sample. 3) In a paired experiment, probes keep 96–101% of their within-language AUROC when the question stem is changed from Polish to English around unchanged entity names, indicating robustness to prompt language. 4) In Gemma-4-12B, manipulating the one-dimensional familiarity direction at a single layer monotonically controls refusal behavior: well-known entities shift refusal rates from 0.24 up to 1.00, while unknown entities shift from 0.73 down to 0.00. 5) A calibrated familiarity probe performs competitively as a pre-generation abstention gate, but post-generation detectors still better predict behavioral errors on average.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides evidence that language models encode a graded notion of entity familiarity in their internal activations, which can be read out before generation using simple probes. This familiarity signal is distinct from the policy that governs when models abstain, but it can be used to influence that policy, as shown by causal interventions along a learned familiarity direction. Polish adaptation plays a key role in how well familiarity tracks real-world popularity, and the signal is relatively robust to prompt language. While not outperforming post-generation detectors for error prediction, pre-generation familiarity probes offer a practical and interpretable tool for abstention and safety in multi-language settings.

title: AI advice suppresses people's willingness to say "I don't know", even when the advice is wrong and accuracy is incentivized
summary: Knowing when to say "I don't know" is fundamental to human judgment, yet AI assistants offer a fluent answer to almost any question. In five experiments (N = 3,132; four preregistered, one direct replication), participants answered difficult questions and could always decline to respond. We engineered the questions so that AI advice was wrong, separating AI use from its accuracy. Merely having access to AI nearly eliminated participants' willingness to suspend judgment, and this held whether the advice was actively requested or simply displayed. Consequently, participants answered more questions but were correct about a third as often as when AI was unavailable-yet their confidence nearly doubled. Incentivizing accuracy and penalizing inaccuracy led participants to seek and follow AI advice less, answer more accurately, and suspend judgment more often, though still far less than when AI was unavailable. As AI suggestions grow ubiquitous and unsolicited, they may not simply affect answer accuracy; they may even alter the metacognitive threshold at which people decide whether they know enough to answer.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how access to AI advice affects people’s willingness to say “I don’t know,” their accuracy, and their confidence on hard questions.
AI.motivation: Humans recognize when they don’t know, but current AI assistants give confident answers to almost anything. The authors want to understand how this contrast affects human judgment: does easy access to AI undermine people’s inclination to suspend judgment, even when the AI is wrong?
AI.method: Across five experiments (total N = 3,132; four preregistered and one direct replication), participants answered difficult questions with the option to decline. The questions were constructed so that the AI’s advice was systematically wrong, allowing the authors to cleanly separate use of AI from its accuracy. Conditions varied whether AI advice was available, whether it had to be requested versus passively displayed, and whether participants were incentivized for accuracy and penalized for inaccuracy.
AI.result: When AI advice was available (even though it was wrong), participants became much less willing to say “I don’t know.” They answered more questions but were only about one-third as accurate as participants without AI access, while their confidence nearly doubled.
