Introducing incentives for accuracy and penalties for inaccuracy reduced reliance on the AI, improved accuracy, and increased the rate of suspending judgment, but people still said “I don’t know” far less often than when AI was unavailable.
AI.conclusion: AI advice, even when incorrect, changes not only what answers people give but also their metacognitive threshold for deciding whether they know enough to answer at all. As AI suggestions become more common and unsolicited, they risk eroding people’s willingness to suspend judgment, inflating confidence while reducing accuracy. Incentive structures can partially mitigate these effects but do not fully restore cautious metacognition to pre‑AI levels.

title: Memory as a Controlled Process: Learned Adaptive Memory Management for LLM Agents
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on external memory systems to accumulate experience across tasks. Yet nearly all existing approaches, from graph-structured memories to reflective insight stores, access memory through fixed, hand-designed heuristics. We argue that this static view of memory is a core bottleneck for agentic learning because optimal memory behavior is fundamentally context-dependent. The early stages of the tasks, benefit from minimal retrieval because memory is sparse; recurring goal types benefit from plan reuse rather than generic nearest-neighbor lookup; stuck agents benefit from re-retrieval with alternative queries; and across long task streams, the memory store itself must be consolidated and pruned to remain useful. We present Memory as a Controlled Process (MemCon), a framework that models memory operations as a Markov Decision Process and learns an online policy that adaptively decides when, what, and how much to retrieve, when to inject a distilled plan, and when to consolidate or forget. MemCon is backend-agnostic: it wraps any existing memory implementation, learns from task-by-task binary feedback with no pretraining and no additional LLM calls, and uses a lightweight tabular contextual bandit with UCB exploration that converges within tens of tasks. Across 6 benchmarks, 3 agent frameworks, and 3 LLM backbones, MemCon consistently outperforms multiple memory baselines by up to 15.2 points in task success while reducing token consumption by 5--20%.

AI.tldr: They propose MemCon, a learnable controller for LLM agents’ memories, which decides when and how to retrieve, reuse, and prune memory, improving success rates and reducing token use.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-agent memory systems use fixed, hand-designed retrieval and update rules (e.g., nearest-neighbor retrieval, fixed reflection schedules). These static heuristics ignore that optimal memory behavior is highly context-dependent: early in a task little memory is useful; for recurring goals, full-plan reuse is better than generic retrieval; stuck agents need alternative queries; long task streams require consolidation and forgetting. This rigidity limits agentic learning and efficiency.
AI.method: They formalize memory usage as a Markov Decision Process and introduce MemCon, which learns an online policy over memory operations. The controller decides: when/what/how much to retrieve; when to insert a distilled plan instead of raw memories; and when to consolidate or forget items. MemCon is designed to be backend-agnostic and wraps any existing memory store. It learns from simple binary task-level feedback (success/failure), uses a lightweight tabular contextual bandit algorithm with UCB exploration, and requires no extra LLM calls or pretraining. The policy converges within tens of tasks.
AI.result: On 6 benchmarks, across 3 different agent frameworks and 3 different LLM backbones, MemCon beats multiple strong memory baselines by as much as 15.2 percentage points in task success rate. At the same time, it cuts token consumption by roughly 5–20%, demonstrating both effectiveness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Treating memory as a controllable, learnable process rather than a fixed heuristic significantly improves LLM agents. A simple bandit-based controller that optimizes when and how to use and modify memory can generalize across agents and models, yielding higher task success and lower token costs without extra supervision or model calls.

title: SAFETY SENTRY: Context-Aware Human Intervention via EXECUTE-ASK-REFUSE Routing
summary: LLM agents act on real-world environments through tool calls, and a single misjudged action can cause irreversible harm. The standard safeguard is a guard model that labels each proposed action as safe or unsafe, but this binary view conflates two distinct decisions: whether the action is harmful in itself, and whether it is appropriate given the user's context. It also operates at the granularity of action categories rather than individual instances, producing routine interruptions that erode autonomy and train users to wave through the most consequential alerts. We reframe the problem as a per-instance three-way routing decision over {EXECUTE, ASK, REFUSE} and instantiate it with Safety Sentry, a lightweight guard model whose inference reduces to a single decoding call. A single decoding-time threshold lets one fixed checkpoint be re-positioned across deployments of differing risk tolerance without retraining. Safety Sentry outperforms a broad set of open-weight and frontier closed-source baselines on overall accuracy and safety-related recall, while controlling both directional error rates simultaneously.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Safety Sentry, a guard model for LLM agents that makes per-instance three-way routing decisions (EXECUTE, ASK, REFUSE) to reduce harm from misjudged tool calls while preserving autonomy.
AI.motivation: LLM agents can cause irreversible real-world harm via misjudged tool calls. Existing safeguards use a binary safe/unsafe guard that conflates intrinsic harmfulness with contextual appropriateness and works at coarse action-category granularity, leading to frequent, low-quality interruptions that users learn to ignore, weakening safety.
AI.method: Reframe safety as a per-instance three-way routing task over {EXECUTE, ASK, REFUSE} for each proposed tool call. Implement this with Safety Sentry, a lightweight guard model whose decision is produced in a single decoding call. Design the model so that a single inference-time threshold can tune its sensitivity to risk, allowing the same checkpoint to be reused in deployments with different risk tolerances without retraining. Evaluate against multiple open-weight and closed-source baselines.
AI.result: Safety Sentry achieves higher overall accuracy and better recall on safety-related cases than a broad set of baseline guard models, while simultaneously keeping both types of errors (overblocking and underblocking) under control. The three-way routing improves the match between intervention level and actual risk of each individual action instance.
AI.conclusion: Per-instance three-way routing (EXECUTE, ASK, REFUSE) is a more effective and flexible framing for safeguarding LLM agents than binary safe/unsafe labels. Safety Sentry operationalizes this framing with a lightweight, threshold-tunable guard model that outperforms existing baselines, offers better controllability of risk tolerance across deployments, and reduces unnecessary interruptions without sacrificing safety.

title: Analogical Deep Research: Retrieving and Integrating Historical Analogies for Foresight Analysis
summary: Systematic comparisons between current situations and structurally similar past events in the historical, i.e., historical analogies, is among the most powerful tools for foresight analysis. In this work, we present a new task called Analogical Deep Research (ADR) to Large Language Model (LLM) agents and construct the first ADR benchmark ADR-bench to study whether LLM agents are able to find and leverage historical analogies when doing foresight analysis. Our investigation reveals a key obstacle: LLM agents are poor at finding analogies because they match on surface features rather than underlying mechanisms. We argue that ADR is inherently a causal question as it requires understanding why the event occurred. Based on our theoretical analysis, we propose two principles required for ADR, including the mechanism alignment and cross-analogy confirmation. Built upon our theoretical results, we propose a new agentic framework called Causal Analogical Researcher (CANA) that guides LLMs to find and integrate historical analogies. CANA incorporates a simple yet effective structural decomposition representation, and integrates structural feedback for reflective improvements of historical analogy identification and integration. We show that CANA brings up to 10% improvements in historical analogy generation, and surpasses the state-of-the-art deep research agents in the ADR-bench. Case studies with the ongoing events confirm the effectiveness of CANA in leveraging historical analogies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Analogical Deep Research (ADR) and a benchmark (ADR-bench) to test whether LLM agents can use historical analogies for foresight, and proposes a new agent framework, Causal Analogical Researcher (CANA), that improves analogy quality and outperforms existing agents.
AI.motivation: Historical analogies are powerful for understanding current events and forecasting future developments, but existing LLM agents tend to match events based on superficial similarity rather than deeper causal mechanisms. There is no established benchmark to rigorously evaluate and improve LLMs’ ability to find and use such analogies for foresight analysis.
AI.method: 1) Formulate the new task Analogical Deep Research (ADR) for LLM agents, which requires discovering and leveraging historical analogies for foresight. 2) Build ADR-bench, the first benchmark specifically designed to evaluate this capability. 3) Theoretically analyze ADR as a causal reasoning problem and derive two key principles: mechanism alignment (matching underlying causal mechanisms, not surface features) and cross-analogy confirmation (using multiple analogies to validate causal hypotheses). 4) Design the Causal Analogical Researcher (CANA) framework, which uses structural decomposition to represent events, guides LLMs to search for and align causal mechanisms, and provides structural feedback loops for reflective improvement in analogy identification and integration.
AI.result: Experiments on ADR-bench show that CANA improves the quality of historical analogy generation by up to 10% compared with baselines and outperforms state-of-the-art deep research agents across benchmark metrics. Qualitative case studies on ongoing events further demonstrate that CANA can identify more appropriate historical analogies and use them more effectively in foresight analysis than existing systems.
AI.conclusion: Analogical Deep Research is a fundamentally causal reasoning problem that standard LLM agents handle poorly when relying on surface similarity. By enforcing mechanism alignment and cross-analogy confirmation via the CANA framework with structural decomposition and feedback, LLM agents can substantially improve their use of historical analogies for foresight, as validated by ADR-bench and real-world case studies.

title: Automatic Ordinary Differential Equations Discovery For Biological Systems Using Large Language Model Powered Agentic System
summary: Automatic scientific discovery has long been a goal of computational scholars - a machine that can discover nature's secrets on its own, moving computational systems beyond data-fitting tools toward the generation and refinement of mechanistic models of the universe. Recent advances in symbolic regression (SR) and large-language-model (LLM)-based agents suggest that such systems can recover equations from data, incorporate domain priors, and automate parts of the research workflow. However, most existing approaches either focus on narrow equation-discovery benchmarks or broad end-to-end automation pipelines, while biological systems remain comparatively underexplored. Here, we introduce the MEDA system, an LLM- and SR-powered agentic framework for discovering ordinary-differential-equation (ODE) models of biological and biologically inspired dynamical systems. MEDA retrieves background knowledge, defines admissible variables, generates mechanistic constraints, proposes candidate ODEs, and fits and evaluates them. We evaluate it across canonical model retrieval, reasoning-based extrapolation to unseen variants, and open-ended discovery, with and without experimental data. Across these settings, MEDA recovered the correct state variables, achieved strong structural recovery in retrieval and extrapolation tasks, and produced biologically plausible discovery-oriented models. Ablation and robustness analyses show that knowledge-guided formalization and mechanistic constraints are load-bearing components, whereas numerical fitting alone can preserve trajectory-compatible but biologically incorrect equations.

AI.tldr: They present MEDA, an LLM + symbolic-regression framework that automatically discovers ODE models for biological dynamical systems, showing it can retrieve known models, extrapolate to variants, and propose plausible new mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Automatic scientific discovery remains limited: current symbolic regression tools mostly solve narrow equation-recovery benchmarks, and LLM-based agents focus on broad workflow automation rather than mechanistic modeling, especially in biology. There is a need for systems that can move beyond data fitting to generate, constrain, and validate mechanistic ODE models of complex biological dynamics using both data and prior knowledge.
AI.method: They build MEDA, an agentic pipeline that integrates large language models with symbolic regression to construct ODE models. MEDA retrieves relevant background knowledge, identifies and defines admissible state variables, uses the LLM to generate mechanistic and structural constraints, proposes candidate ODE forms via SR under those constraints, and then fits and evaluates these equations against data. They test MEDA on tasks including canonical model retrieval, reasoning-driven extrapolation to unseen model variants, and open-ended model discovery, in scenarios with and without experimental data. They also perform ablations and robustness checks to isolate the contributions of knowledge-guided formalization and mechanistic constraints versus pure numerical fitting.
AI.result: MEDA correctly recovered the relevant state variables and showed strong structural recovery of the underlying equations in both retrieval and extrapolation tasks. In more open-ended discovery settings, it generated models considered biologically plausible. The ablation studies reveal that incorporating background knowledge into the problem formalization and enforcing mechanistic constraints are crucial to success, while relying mainly on numerical fitting can still match observed trajectories but often yields biologically wrong or misleading equations.
AI.conclusion: An LLM- and SR-based agentic framework like MEDA can meaningfully advance automatic scientific discovery in biology by going beyond pure curve fitting to generate and refine mechanistic ODE models. Proper use of domain knowledge and explicit mechanistic constraints is essential; without them, systems risk finding equations that fit data but misrepresent underlying biology. The work demonstrates feasibility on several biological dynamical systems and highlights the importance of knowledge-guided structure in automated equation discovery.

title: Self-Evolving Agent Harnesses via Gated Semantic Quality-Diversity
summary: An LLM agent's real-task performance is shaped as much by the harness around its model as by the frozen model itself: its prompts, injected knowledge, runtime control, and configuration. In deployment the harness is often the only lever available, so improving it automatically is the natural way to raise performance without touching the weights. The hard part is not generating changes but knowing which one truly helped. Self-generated feedback is noisy, and an apparent gain can be a measurement artifact or an edit that merely overfits the tasks it was tuned on. We present a self-evolving agent-harness framework that separates proposing changes from crediting them: a language model diagnoses failures and proposes patches, while all sampling, measurement, and significance testing are owned by deterministic code, so every credited improvement is trustworthy by construction. Patches populate a gated, categorical quality-diversity archive (GSME) keyed on the (WHERE x WHY) pathology an edit addresses rather than the tasks it fixes, an anti-overfitting inductive bias; generalization is measured on a sealed test scored only after evolution. Across seven domains with a frozen open-weight model, the harness is train-selected and scored once on a sealed test; its credited gains there are +9 to +15.5pp and retain 86-147% of the training gain, evidence they generalize rather than overfit. The winning patch tracks the model's dominant pathology, not its size or family: changing the model can change the pathology and the patch, while the same pathology-to-patch match recurs across two model families. What transfers is the diagnose-and-credit loop, not any specific harness.

AI.tldr: They build a self-evolving framework that automatically improves LLM agent harnesses (prompts, tools, configs) via trusted, statistically tested patches that generalize rather than overfit.
AI.motivation: LLM agents’ real-world performance depends heavily on the surrounding harness (prompts, tools, configs), which is usually the only thing practitioners can change at deployment time. Current attempts to auto-improve harnesses struggle because self-evaluation is noisy and changes may just overfit to tuning tasks or measurement artifacts rather than truly improving general performance.
AI.method: They separate the creative and evaluative roles: a language model only diagnoses failures and proposes harness patches, while deterministic code handles sampling, evaluation, and significance testing to ensure that credited improvements are statistically reliable. Patches are stored in a gated, categorical quality-diversity archive keyed by the type of pathology they target (WHERE in the pipeline and WHY it fails), rather than by tasks, to reduce overfitting. Evolution and selection are done on training tasks while a sealed test set is used only once at the end to measure generalization.
AI.result: Across seven domains using a frozen open-weight LLM, their evolving harness achieves test-time performance gains of +9 to +15.5 percentage points, and these gains preserve 86–147% of the improvements seen on training tasks, indicating strong generalization beyond the tuning distribution. The top-performing patch consistently addresses the dominant failure mode of the model; when the underlying model changes, the dominant pathology and thus the winning patch change, but the same pathology-to-patch patterns recur across two different model families.
AI.conclusion: Automatically evolving the harness around a frozen LLM using a diagnose-and-credit loop can reliably and significantly improve performance without modifying model weights. By decoupling patch proposal from deterministic, statistically grounded credit assignment and organizing patches by pathology rather than task, the framework finds improvements that generalize instead of overfitting. The transferable component is the evolution procedure itself, not any specific harness configuration.

title: STOCKTAKE: Measuring the Gap Between Perception and Action in LLM Agents with a Fair Oracle
summary: LLM agents are increasingly evaluated on multi-week decision tasks in which the state that drives cost is never directly observed. On such tasks the final cost cannot say why an agent failed: it may have misread the world, or read it correctly and still failed to act (the knowing-doing gap). Existing evaluations cannot separate these two failures; their reference policies either read privileged information the agent never sees, or are missing altogether. We introduce STOCKTAKE, a 26-week supply-chain replenishment benchmark built as a factored partially observable Markov decision process with six hidden factor processes, designed so that a fair reference policy is computable: an exact Bayes filter per factor drives a rollout policy on the identical observation stream the agent receives. Scoring each run between a symptom-blind base-stock floor (0) and this oracle (1) yields a skill score, and grading each week's written rationale yields a stated-belief detection lag and a knowing-doing rate, so state estimation and control are measured separately. On fifty seeds with curated stress profiles, Claude Sonnet 5, GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, and Grok 4.5 detect 84-88% of hidden failures, typically within a week of onset, yet span skill scores from 0.62 to -0.23: two of the four end below the symptom-blind floor while naming factors slightly faster than the two that beat it. The failure has two faces. Where stress persists, 34-43% of correctly diagnosed stress weeks still end in stockout for every model, a rate that partly reflects the severity of the weeks models notice. That rate also runs opposite to skill: the two models under the floor stock out least on diagnosed weeks, so under-response is only one face of the gap, and their traces point to the other, responses whose cost exceeds what they protect. STOCKTAKE measures both directions of that failure.

AI.tldr: Introduces STOCKTAKE, a long-horizon supply-chain benchmark that cleanly separates LLM agents’ state-estimation ability from their control/decision quality.
AI.motivation: Current long-horizon evaluations of LLM agents on partially observed tasks cannot disentangle two distinct failure modes: (1) misperceiving or mis-estimating the hidden state of the world, and (2) correctly understanding the state but choosing poor actions (the knowing–doing gap). Final task cost alone is ambiguous, and existing reference policies are either unfair (using privileged information) or absent. A principled benchmark is needed where a fair oracle policy exists and where both belief tracking and action quality can be independently assessed.
AI.method: They design STOCKTAKE, a 26-week supply-chain replenishment benchmark formalized as a factored partially observable Markov decision process with six hidden factor processes. For each factor, they construct an exact Bayes filter that operates on the same observation stream available to the LLM agent. This drives an oracle rollout policy that serves as a fair reference. Performance on each run is normalized between a symptom-blind base-stock heuristic (score 0) and the oracle (score 1) to yield a skill score. In addition, they collect and grade the agent’s weekly natural-language rationales to extract when it first explicitly recognizes each hidden failure (stated-belief detection lag) and how often correct diagnoses are followed by appropriate actions (knowing–doing rate). They evaluate several cutting-edge LLM agents (Claude Sonnet 5, GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Grok 4.5) on 50 scenario seeds with curated stress profiles.
AI.result: Across models, 84–88% of hidden failures are eventually detected, usually within about one week of onset, showing that state estimation via language reasoning is relatively strong. However, skill scores vary widely, from 0.62 down to -0.23, with two models performing worse than the symptom-blind baseline despite diagnosing issues slightly faster than the better-performing models. Furthermore, even when stress is correctly diagnosed, 34–43% of those weeks still end in stockouts for every model. The pattern is not monotone: the two below-baseline models actually stock out least often on diagnosed weeks, suggesting they are more conservative, whereas the better-scoring models sometimes take costly overreactions. This reveals that both under-response and over-response (actions whose costs exceed their benefits) contribute to the knowing–doing gap.
AI.conclusion: STOCKTAKE provides a structured way to independently measure LLM agents’ hidden-state inference and decision quality on long-horizon, partially observed tasks. It shows that current leading models can usually recognize emerging supply-chain stresses but still frequently fail to translate that knowledge into cost-effective actions, with both insufficient and excessive responses degrading performance. The benchmark’s fair oracle, normalized skill metric, and rationale-based diagnostics make it a useful tool for analyzing and improving LLM agents’ decision-making under uncertainty.

title: The Test Oracle Problem in Synthetic LLM-as-Judge Corpora: Disappearance, Distortion and a Validation Protocol
summary: Studies of bias in LLM-as-judge systems typically build synthetic corpora by prompting an LLM to generate a hallucinated answer to pair with a factual one, then presenting both to a judge. We report a case in which this generation step silently failed, and use it to argue that the failure mode is structural rather than incidental. In a multilingual (Turkish/English) faithfulness-judgment corpus, a decoding-budget parameter shared between judging and generation calls truncated one producer's hallucinated answers to a few words. The resulting items produced a large, statistically robust effect: a 32-point cross-lingual collapse in one judge's selection accuracy, replicated from N=50 to N=500, explained by a three-layer mechanistic account, and confirmed by a controlled producer-swap experiment, none of which was real. The effect vanished to ceiling once the shared parameter was corrected, and only manual reading of the raw generations, not any aggregate statistical check, exposed the fault. A second measured bias (Markdown-formatting preference) was not fabricated but distorted by the same fault, its magnitude and in one case its sign shifting with stimulus length, a mode aggregate metrics cannot distinguish from the first. We frame the underlying vulnerability using the test oracle problem: corpora whose negative examples are LLM-generated carry no mechanical way to verify item integrity, while corpora built by deterministic perturbation of a gold answer carry an item-level oracle for free. A positive control supports this claim directly: an analogous fault injected into a minimal perturbation-based corpus is caught with 100% accuracy by a zero-cost, zero-human gold-to-negative string comparison. We close with a validation protocol, derived from our own case, for analysts working in the oracle-less regime that we argue describes most contemporary multilingual LLM-as-judge corpora.

AI.tldr: The paper exposes a structural vulnerability in LLM-as-judge bias studies that use LLM-generated hallucinations as negatives, showing how a silent failure in generation fabricated a large, seemingly robust cross-lingual bias effect.
AI.motivation: To question the reliability and validity of bias measurements in LLM-as-judge setups that rely on LLM-generated negative examples, and to show how silent generation failures can produce strong but spurious empirical findings.
AI.method: They analyze a multilingual (Turkish/English) faithfulness-judgment corpus where a shared decoding-budget parameter accidentally truncated hallucinated answers. They examine the apparent bias effect via replication (N=50 to N=500), mechanistic analysis, a producer-swap experiment, then correct the parameter to show the effect disappears. They contrast this with a perturbation-based corpus and run a positive-control fault-injection test, where a simple string comparison reliably catches corrupted items.
AI.result: The shared decoding-budget caused producer hallucinations to be truncated to a few words, fabricating a 32-point cross-lingual drop in accuracy plus distortion of an observed Markdown-formatting bias. When the bug was fixed, the effects vanished or changed substantially. In contrast, an analogous bug in a perturbation-based corpus was perfectly detectable via automatic gold-vs-negative comparison.
AI.conclusion: LLM-as-judge corpora that use LLM-generated negatives lack a built-in oracle to verify item integrity, making them highly vulnerable to silent generation faults that can yield strong but spurious bias effects. Perturbation-based datasets have a natural oracle and are more robust. The authors propose a validation protocol for researchers working with oracle-less, especially multilingual, LLM-as-judge corpora.

title: UESF-Bench: Benchmarking and Probing for Unified Embodied Seeking and Following
summary: Language-guided human following is an important capability for embodied agents, but existing benchmarks typically assume that the target person is visible at the start of an episode. This setting simplifies the problem and overlooks a more realistic requirement: an agent often needs to first find a language-described target and then persistently follow that target in a dynamic environment. While recent work has started to study human search, existing settings are typically evaluated in task-specific scenarios and often rely on stronger prior knowledge of the environment. Moreover, they usually treat searching and following as separate tasks and still lack a unified benchmark for systematic evaluation. To address these limitations, we introduce the Unified Embodied Seeking and Following Benchmark (UESF-Bench), a large-scale and diverse benchmark for embodied human seeking and following. The benchmark requires agents to handle semantic-guided exploration, reliable behavior switching and recovery, and delayed identity grounding. To this end, we propose SeekFollow-VLA, a vision-language-action framework with a task-driven routing mechanism for latent phase inference and transition modeling between seeking and following. Experimental results show that SeekFollow-VLA achieves clear improvements over both single-head and dual-head baselines across single-person and multi-person environments, establishing a baseline for unified embodied seek-and-follow.

AI.tldr: They introduce a new benchmark and model for robots that must first find, then keep following, a person described in language, in realistic, dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Existing language-guided human-following benchmarks assume the target is already visible, which is unrealistic. Human search tasks studied so far are narrow, environment-specific, often depend on strong prior knowledge, and treat searching and following as separate tasks. There is no unified, systematic benchmark for the full “seek then follow” problem.
AI.method: They create UESF-Bench, a large-scale, diverse benchmark requiring agents to do semantic-guided exploration, switch reliably between seeking and following, recover from failures, and ground a person’s identity after delay. They also propose SeekFollow-VLA, a vision–language–action architecture with a task-driven routing mechanism that infers the current phase (seeking vs following) in latent space and models transitions between these phases, unifying the two behaviors.
AI.result: On UESF-Bench, SeekFollow-VLA significantly outperforms both single-head and dual-head baseline models, in both single-person and multi-person environments. This demonstrates better search–follow integration and robustness than prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: UESF-Bench provides the first unified benchmark for embodied language-guided seeking and following, enabling systematic evaluation of this compound task. SeekFollow-VLA serves as a strong initial baseline, showing that explicitly modeling latent phases and transitions between seeking and following yields clear performance gains in realistic, dynamic settings.

title: Self-supervised Speech Comparison for L2 Phone, Rhythm, and Intonation Scoring
summary: L2 speech assessment has traditionally focused on phonetic assessment, leaving the scoring of suprasegmental features such as rhythm and intonation underexplored. Moreover, assessment methods often require training with labeled L2 speech data, making them difficult to apply in low-resource settings. We investigate whether DTW over self-supervised WavLM representations can provide a text-free framework for assessing phonetic accuracy, rhythm, and intonation in English and Japanese L2 speech. Results show that a basic DTW-based approach that compares learner speech to native templates exceeds human agreement on holistic and sentence-level phonetic scoring. For rhythm, we introduce methods that measure the degree of warping in the DTW alignment path; our best method approaches human-level performance. For intonation, we combine DTW distance over prosodic residuals with pitch and intensity features, but performance remains more modest on some tasks. Our results point to self-supervised representations as a promising, text-free basis for multi-aspect pronunciation assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a text-free, template-based method for assessing multiple aspects of L2 pronunciation (phonetic accuracy, rhythm, intonation) using DTW over self-supervised WavLM representations, achieving near or above human agreement on several scoring tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing L2 speech assessment focuses largely on segmental (phonetic) features and underexplores suprasegmental aspects like rhythm and intonation. Many current methods require labeled L2 speech data, limiting applicability in low-resource scenarios. The authors aim to develop an automatic, multi-aspect pronunciation assessment method that does not rely on transcriptions or labeled learner data and can work across languages (English and Japanese).
AI.method: They use self-supervised WavLM acoustic representations and apply Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) between L2 learner utterances and native-speaker template utterances in a text-free manner. For phonetic accuracy, they compute DTW distances between learner and native templates. For rhythm, they derive warping-based metrics from the DTW alignment path that capture temporal distortions. For intonation, they compute DTW distances over prosodic residuals and incorporate pitch and intensity features. They then evaluate these metrics by correlating them with human ratings at holistic and sentence levels for English and Japanese L2 speech.
AI.result: A simple DTW-based comparison between learner and native speech using WavLM features yields automatic phonetic scores that exceed human inter-rater agreement for holistic and sentence-level phonetic accuracy. Rhythm assessment using warping-path-based measures achieves performance close to human-level agreement. Intonation assessment combining DTW over prosodic residuals with pitch and intensity provides reasonable but weaker performance, especially on some tasks, compared to human ratings.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised WavLM representations, when combined with DTW alignment to native templates, offer a promising, text-free approach for automatic assessment of multiple aspects of L2 pronunciation, including phonetic accuracy and rhythm, with near- or above-human reliability. Intonation assessment is more challenging and remains an area for improvement, but overall the framework appears suitable for low-resource settings where labeled data and transcripts are scarce.

title: Explaining Reinforcement Learning Agents via Inductive Logic Programming
summary: Explainable Reinforcement Learning (XRL) seeks to make Reinforcement Learning (RL) policies more transparent and interpretable, a key requirement in safety-critical and human-centric scenarios. However, it is mostly based on user studies, thus targeting the needs of a specific audience and lacking shared evaluation metrics. On the other hand, logic-based approaches within eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) provide compact, human-readable abstractions of decision-making. However, the systematic quantification of the explainability degree of logical representations remains an open problem. This work aims to advance the state of the art in XRL by introducing objective and planning-oriented metrics for policy explainability in RL settings. At the same time, it contributes to the field of logic for XAI by providing a principled way to quantify the explainability of logical rules, moving beyond common-sense assessments and simple propositional fragments. We employ Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) to extract symbolic representations of RL policies and define a novel set of explainability metrics, including activation rate, feature coverage, syntactic distance and semantic distance. These metrics quantify alignment between symbolic rules and agent behavior, the role of features in decision-making, and the evolution of policies during training and across agents in single and multi-agent RL. Experiments across different RL domains show that the proposed metrics highlight action-specific learning dynamics beyond global return, provide fine-grained insights into domain features beyond classical approaches for global feature importance estimation, and uncover coordination, specialization, and adaptation patterns in MARL. Moreover, they provide crucial insights for the transfer and generalization of action-specific policies.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes objective, logic-based metrics to evaluate how explainable reinforcement learning policies are, using rules learned via Inductive Logic Programming.
AI.motivation: Current explainable reinforcement learning mostly relies on user studies tailored to specific audiences and lacks shared, objective evaluation metrics. Logic-based XAI can produce human-readable rules, but there is no systematic way to quantify how explainable these logical representations are. The authors want to address both gaps: provide general, planning-oriented explainability metrics for RL policies and a principled way to measure the explainability of logical rules.
AI.method: The authors use Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) to extract symbolic (logical rule) representations of reinforcement learning policies. On top of these rule sets, they define a suite of explainability metrics: activation rate (how often rules fire), feature coverage (which and how many features appear in rules), syntactic distance (how different rules are syntactically), and semantic distance (how different rules are in behavior/meaning). These metrics are applied to single-agent and multi-agent RL to study policy behavior, feature roles, and policy evolution over training.
AI.result: Across several RL domains, the metrics reveal detailed properties of learned policies that are not visible from standard performance measures like return. They expose action-specific learning dynamics, finer-grained information about which features matter compared to standard global feature-importance methods, and coordination/specialization/adaptation patterns in multi-agent RL policies. The metrics also illuminate how action-specific policies transfer and generalize between settings.
AI.conclusion: Objective, logic-based explainability metrics built on ILP-extracted rules can systematically quantify multiple facets of RL policy explainability. These metrics go beyond subjective user studies and simple propositional analyses, offering richer insights into agent behavior, feature roles, multi-agent coordination, and policy transfer and generalization. This advances both explainable RL and logic-based XAI by providing a shared, principled evaluation framework for explainability.

title: Post-Training Shifts Confidence: A Three-Stage Analysis of How SFT, RL, and OPD Shape Pre-, Intra-, and Post-CoT Calibration
summary: Large language models have made strong reasoning gains through supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and on-policy distillation, yet these post-training methods are usually evaluated only by final-answer accuracy. We study how they reshape confidence during reasoning. We introduce a three-stage calibration framework that evaluates confidence before, during, and after chain-of-thought generation, corresponding to difficulty estimation, early termination, and answer aggregation. Through a controlled comparison on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, we find that OPD provides the most useful pre-reasoning confidence, SFT gives the strongest online signal for early stopping, and RL produces the most reliable trace-level signal for aggregation. We further show that confidence reliability is position-dependent: RL confidence becomes informative after a path-commitment phase, while OPD confidence is useful early but can become inversely calibrated later. Based on this observation, we propose PosConf, a position-aware confidence strategy that uses confidence only from reliable relative-position intervals. PosConf improves RL answer aggregation by 6.1 points over majority voting and consistently improves OPD early stopping under tight token budgets, with gains up to 4.3 points by avoiding its later inverse-calibration region, showing that \emph{confidence in reasoning models should be used both stage-wise and position-awarely}. Our code is available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/Post-Training-Calibration.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how post-training methods for large language models affect the calibration of model confidence at different stages of chain-of-thought reasoning, and proposes a position-aware strategy (PosConf) that leverages only reliability-tested portions of the confidence signal to improve answer aggregation and early stopping.
AI.motivation: Most work on post-training LLMs (SFT, RL, on-policy distillation) focuses on improving final-answer accuracy, largely ignoring how these methods affect the quality and calibration of the model’s confidence during the reasoning process itself. In practice, reliable confidence is crucial for tasks like deciding when to stop generation, estimating problem difficulty, and aggregating multiple reasoning traces. The authors want to understand and improve how different post-training schemes shape confidence over the full chain-of-thought, not just at the final output.
AI.method: The authors build a three-stage calibration framework that measures confidence (1) before reasoning starts (difficulty estimation), (2) during chain-of-thought generation (for potential early termination), and (3) after multiple reasoning traces are generated (for answer aggregation). They conduct controlled experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks comparing supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning (RL), and on-policy distillation (OPD), analyzing how well each method’s confidence scores correlate with correctness at different positions in the reasoning traces. Observing that confidence reliability varies with position along the chain-of-thought, they design PosConf, a position-aware strategy that selectively uses confidence only from intervals where it is empirically well-calibrated for each post-training method and stage.
AI.result: Empirically, OPD models provide the best calibrated confidence before reasoning starts (useful for difficulty estimation), SFT models give the strongest and most reliable online confidence for early stopping, and RL models yield the most trustworthy trace-level confidence for aggregating multiple solutions. The authors further discover that confidence calibration is position-dependent: RL’s confidence becomes reliable only after a “path-commitment” phase in the chain-of-thought, whereas OPD’s confidence is initially good but later becomes inversely calibrated. Using these insights, PosConf, which restricts the use of confidence to positions where it is reliable, improves RL-based answer aggregation by 6.1 accuracy points over simple majority voting, and improves OPD early stopping performance under strict token budgets by up to 4.3 points by avoiding the poorly calibrated later positions.
AI.conclusion: Post-training techniques reshape not only accuracy but also the structure and calibration of confidence over the entire reasoning trajectory. Confidence in reasoning LLMs is inherently stage- and position-dependent, so it should not be treated as a uniform signal. By explicitly modeling this dependence and using confidence only in empirically reliable positional intervals (as done in PosConf), systems can significantly improve both answer aggregation and early stopping in chain-of-thought reasoning settings.

title: When Bots Join the Team: Bot Adoption and the Institutional Fabric of Open-Source Software Projects
summary: AI agents are joining human teams, raising a basic question: when an automated agent becomes a regular participant, does group organization strengthen or weaken? We study this question in open-source software, where bots open pull requests, review code, and merge changes alongside people, leaving a public record of every interaction. Treating bots as participants rather than tools, we examine 2,991 GitHub projects for two years before and after each adopted its first bot. We measure three capabilities that institutional theory links to durable coordination - repeated engagement, social memory, and role differentiation - and two outcomes: conflict cascades and output distinctiveness. Bot adoption is followed by more repeated collaboration, greater recognition of specific bots in discussion, fewer conflict cascades, and more distinctive outputs. These changes cluster around adoption rather than accumulating gradually. Because we lack an untreated comparison group, we interpret the results as precisely timed associations, not causal effects. Two patterns are difficult for alternative explanations to account for: capabilities predict outcomes according to their function - coordination versus differentiation - rather than whether humans or bots provide them, and human-side capabilities account for the bot-conflict association but not the bot-distinctiveness association. The findings are consistent with a specific interpretation: predictable, rule-based agents can become part of a community's social infrastructure. The bot is the occasion; social organization is the mechanism.

AI.tldr: Study of how introducing bots as regular participants in open-source GitHub teams relates to coordination and project outcomes.
AI.motivation: As AI bots increasingly work alongside humans, it is unclear whether their participation strengthens or weakens group organization and coordination. Open-source software projects on GitHub provide a rich setting to observe these dynamics because all interactions are publicly logged.
AI.method: Treat bots as participants rather than tools and conduct an observational study of 2,991 GitHub projects, comparing two-year windows before and after each project adopts its first bot. Measure three coordination-related capabilities—repeated engagement, social memory, and role differentiation—and two outcome variables: conflict cascades and output distinctiveness. Analyze changes around the adoption event and examine patterns that align with or challenge alternative explanations, noting that there is no untreated comparison group so results are interpreted as associations, not causal effects.
AI.result: After bot adoption, projects exhibit more repeated collaboration among contributors, greater recognition and referencing of specific bots in discussions, fewer conflict cascades, and more distinctive project outputs. These shifts appear temporally clustered around the adoption of the first bot rather than building up gradually over time.
AI.conclusion: The presence of predictable, rule-based bots can become integrated into a community's social infrastructure and is associated with stronger coordination and more distinctive outputs. The key mechanisms appear to be social and organizational responses to the bot, rather than the bot itself directly causing the observed changes; capabilities align with their functional roles, and human-side capabilities explain reduced conflict but not increased distinctiveness. Bots act as the occasion that restructures social organization, which in turn drives the outcomes.

title: SPyCE: Skill-Policy Co-evolution for Multimodal Agents
summary: Multimodal agents that think with images iteratively manipulate visual evidence and invoke tools across many steps. Existing reinforcement learning methods reduce trajectories to scalar rewards, forcing the policy to discover reusable tool-use patterns from scratch on every new task; memory-based alternatives retain past experience, yet they rely on test-time retrieval, without updating the policy to absorb reusable patterns from that experience. Our key insight is that multimodal reasoning trajectories should be distilled into reusable skills that co-evolve with the policy during training, rather than being consumed as rewards or retrieved from a static store. To this end, we propose SPyCE (Skill-Policy Co-evolution), a framework that distills trajectories into a hierarchical skill library and updates it throughout reinforcement learning. Execution skills capture local visual operations, while workflow skills encode high-level priors that orchestrate tool use. During training, the policy model conditions on retrieved skills to guide its rollouts, while the skill library evolves using valuable rollouts generated by the policy. This creates a closed loop in which improved policies yield better skills, and the evolving skill library, in turn, provides stronger priors for policy rollouts. Experiments across eight benchmarks demonstrate that SPyCE consistently outperforms both RL-based and memory-based baselines. Further analysis reveals that both the hierarchical skill design and the co-evolution mechanism are critical to our design. These results suggest joint skill-policy optimization as a promising paradigm for building capable multimodal agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SPyCE, a framework that turns multimodal reasoning trajectories into reusable skills that co-evolve with a reinforcement learning policy, significantly improving multimodal tool-using agents.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal RL agents that reason with images either compress trajectories into scalar rewards, losing structural information about useful tool-use patterns, or store trajectories in memory that must be retrieved at test time without updating the policy. Both approaches fail to build a growing, reusable repertoire of skills that the policy can directly leverage and internalize. The authors aim to enable agents to systematically distill and accumulate reusable multimodal skills from their own trajectories so that future reasoning can be more efficient and effective.
AI.method: The authors propose SPyCE (Skill-Policy Co-evolution), which maintains a hierarchical skill library that is updated throughout RL training. Trajectories from multimodal reasoning with images and tools are distilled into two levels of skills: (1) execution skills that represent local visual operations; and (2) workflow skills that encode high-level priors over the sequencing and orchestration of tools. During training, the policy conditions on relevant skills retrieved from the library to guide its rollouts, while new high-value rollouts are used to update and refine the skill library. This establishes a closed-loop co-evolution between the policy and skills: the policy improves by using better skills, and the skill library is refined by the policy’s successful trajectories.
AI.result: Across eight multimodal benchmarks, SPyCE consistently outperforms both standard reinforcement learning approaches that only use scalar rewards and memory-based approaches that rely on trajectory retrieval without policy updating. Ablation studies show that removing the hierarchical skill structure or the co-evolution mechanism significantly degrades performance, indicating that both components are essential. The method yields stronger multimodal agents that are better at tool use and visual reasoning over multiple steps.
AI.conclusion: Distilling multimodal reasoning trajectories into a hierarchical, continually updated library of skills and jointly optimizing these skills with the policy leads to more capable multimodal agents than traditional RL or memory-based methods. The co-evolution of skills and policy provides a powerful paradigm for capturing reusable visual and tool-use patterns, suggesting that future multimodal systems should explicitly model and learn such skill structures rather than relying solely on scalar rewards or static memory retrieval.

title: AgentCompass: A Unified Evaluation Infrastructure for Agent Capabilities
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve into autonomous agents, the need for unified evaluation infrastructure becomes critical. However, current evaluation pipelines remain highly fragmented and tightly coupled, hindering reproducibility and causing redundant engineering. To address this, we introduce AgentCompass, an open-source, lightweight, and extensible infrastructure for evaluating LLM-based agents. AgentCompass organizes the evaluation process around three independent components, namely Benchmark, Harness, and Environment, thereby enabling flexible configurations without requiring the reimplementation of complex execution logic. Furthermore, it features a fault-tolerant asynchronous runtime and comprehensive trajectory analysis tools to transparently diagnose nuanced failure modes like reward-hacking. Natively supporting over 20 benchmarks across five capability dimensions, AgentCompass provides the community with a scalable and reproducible infrastructure for advancing agent research.

AI.tldr: AgentCompass is an open-source, modular infrastructure to systematically evaluate LLM-based agents.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation pipelines for LLM-based agents are fragmented, tightly coupled, and hard to reproduce, leading to duplicated engineering and difficulty comparing results.
AI.method: They design AgentCompass with three decoupled components—Benchmark, Harness, and Environment—plus a fault-tolerant asynchronous runtime and trajectory analysis tools. The system is lightweight, extensible, and supports flexible configuration without reimplementing execution logic.
AI.result: AgentCompass natively supports 20+ benchmarks spanning five capability dimensions and can run them in a scalable, reproducible manner while enabling detailed diagnosis of agent failures, including reward-hacking behaviors.
AI.conclusion: AgentCompass offers a unified, extensible, and reproducible evaluation infrastructure that can standardize and accelerate research on LLM-based agents.

title: High-Order Question Generation in a Multilingual Educational Context
comment: This paper was accepted at the 15th edition of the Language Resources and Evaluation Conference (LREC 2026)
summary: Critical thinking is a fundamental skill that helps learners move beyond simple memorization. One way to develop this skill is through high-order questioning. However, crafting such questions remains a challenge for educators, and classroom practices tend to rely on low-order questions. Large Language Models have demonstrated strong capabilities in generating high-order questions, especially when guided by prompts based on Bloom's Taxonomy. Yet, existing research has largely centered on this framework and focused only on English. This study addresses these gaps by introducing prompts grounded in two alternative frameworks: Claim-Evidence-Reasoning and Divergent Questioning within a multilingual context using Basque, Spanish, and English. Results indicate that while both an open-source and a proprietary model rather effectively generate questions in all three languages, only about half of the answerable questions are recognized by teachers as high-order. A positive finding is that the alternative frameworks produce structurally and conceptually varied questions, suggesting they could complement each other and provide viable alternatives to Bloom's Taxonomy.

AI.tldr: Study tests LLM-generated high-order questions in Basque, Spanish, and English using two frameworks beyond Bloom's Taxonomy.
AI.motivation: Critical thinking requires high-order questions, but teachers struggle to create them and current LLM research focuses mostly on Bloom's Taxonomy and English. The paper aims to explore alternative frameworks and languages.
AI.method: Use prompts based on Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) and Divergent Questioning (DQ) to guide both open-source and proprietary LLMs to generate questions in Basque, Spanish, and English, then have teachers evaluate whether questions are high-order and answerable.
AI.result: Both LLMs can generate questions in the three languages, but only about half of the answerable questions are judged high-order by teachers. CER and DQ prompts yield questions that are structurally and conceptually diverse.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are promising tools for multilingual question generation, but their high-order quality is limited. CER and DQ provide complementary, viable alternatives to Bloom-based prompting, enriching the diversity of generated questions.

title: CAVA: Canonical Action Verification and Attestation for Runtime Governance of Agentic AI Systems
comment: 35 pages. Working paper on canonical action verification, runtime governance, semantic pattern detection, and approval-bound action receipts
summary:
Agentic AI systems increasingly act through heterogeneous runtimes: local coding hooks, SDK tools, browser automation, managed-agent traces, API gateways, and workflow engines. A single operational act such as publishing code, changing identity state, moving money, or exporting data may therefore be represented by many incompatible runtime records. This makes a basic governance question difficult to answer: what action was actually approved, what evidence binds the approval to execution, and can an independent verifier reproduce the same action identity later?
  This paper presents Canonical Action Verification and Attestation (CAVA), a runtime-semantics layer for converting heterogeneous agent activity into canonical runtime action objects. CAVA is positioned below Proof-Carrying Agent Actions (PCAA): PCAA defines the deployer-owned route-review-prove governance process, while CAVA defines the stable action object that process governs. The paper formalizes canonical action identity, semantic pattern detection, approval binding, receipt integrity, runtime-portable projection, and optional attestation substrates. We study a reference implementation through a 96-seed, 384-variant benchmark covering semantic equivalence, semantic separation, wrapper bypass, false-positive control, approval binding, receipt reproducibility, attestation tamper detection, runtime portability, semantic pattern detection, policy degradation, and Azure deployment drills. The contribution is a systems formulation of action-level canonicalization and policy-addressable semantic patterns as a necessary substrate for deployer-side AI governance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Canonical Action Verification and Attestation (CAVA), a system for unifying heterogeneous AI agent runtime actions into canonical, verifiable action objects to support robust governance and auditing.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems operate across many incompatible runtimes and logs, making it hard to answer what exact action was approved, how approval is linked to execution, and whether the same action identity can be independently reproduced later. This fragmentation undermines governance, auditability, and safety controls.
AI.method: Introduce CAVA as a runtime-semantics layer that converts diverse agent activity traces into canonical runtime action objects. The paper formalizes concepts such as canonical action identity, semantic pattern detection, approval binding between governance decisions and actions, integrity of action receipts, runtime-portable projections of actions, and optional cryptographic attestation substrates. It then implements a reference system and evaluates it with a 96-seed, 384-variant benchmark covering tests like semantic equivalence/separation, wrapper bypass resistance, false-positive control, approval binding, receipt reproducibility, tamper detection, runtime portability, semantic pattern detection, and policy degradation, including drills on Azure deployment.
AI.result: The reference implementation successfully demonstrates that heterogeneous runtime events can be normalized into canonical action objects while preserving semantics needed for governance. The benchmark shows CAVA can distinguish semantically equivalent vs. distinct actions, resist wrapper bypass, maintain low false positives, robustly bind approvals to actions, reproduce receipts, detect tampering in attested contexts, operate across runtimes, and support semantic pattern-based policies in realistic deployment scenarios such as Azure.
AI.conclusion: Action-level canonicalization plus policy-addressable semantic patterns are a necessary substrate for deployer-side AI governance. CAVA provides a concrete systems architecture and empirical evidence that such canonical action objects can be constructed and used across heterogeneous runtimes, enabling more reliable approval workflows, auditing, and independent verification for agentic AI systems.

title: DeepStress: Stress-Testing Deep Search Agents
summary: While search agents demonstrate impressive capabilities in multi-step question answering, their robustness to poor-quality evidence remains under-explored. This phenomenon occurs rarely in realistic benchmarks but can lead to dramatic failure in real life applications. Therefore in this study we propose DeepStress, a stress testing framework that controls the frequency of challenging evidence by replacing the retrieval module of search agents with a controlled synthetic environment. We use this framework to control three dimensions that can affect document reliability: trustworthiness, relevance, and factuality. Testing several search agents on HotpotQA and BrowseCompPlus, we demonstrate that agents exhibit substantial differences in their ability to handle unreliable information and propose new metrics that better document systems outcomes as well as the interactions between conflicting parametric and retrieved knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DeepStress, a framework to systematically stress-test search agents by exposing them to controlled amounts of low-quality or misleading evidence.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-step QA benchmarks rarely contain much poor-quality or misleading evidence, so search agents look strong there, but may fail badly in real-world scenarios with unreliable documents. Robustness to such low-quality evidence is underexplored and needs targeted evaluation.
AI.method: The authors replace the normal retrieval module in search agents with a synthetic, controllable environment (DeepStress). Within this environment they can vary and control three key dimensions of document reliability—trustworthiness, relevance, and factuality—so they can precisely inject challenging evidence and observe system behavior.
AI.result: Using DeepStress on HotpotQA and BrowseCompPlus, the authors show that different search agents behave very differently when confronted with unreliable or conflicting information. They also observe complex interactions between the models’ internal (parametric) knowledge and the external retrieved content.
AI.conclusion: Robustness to unreliable evidence is not well captured by standard benchmarks, and search agents can fail sharply when evidence quality degrades. DeepStress provides a way to systematically probe this vulnerability and reveals significant performance and behavioral differences across agents, motivating the use of new, more diagnostic metrics for evaluating search-based QA systems.

title: Experience Memory Graph: One-Shot Error Correction for Agents
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown remarkable capabilities in autonomous decision-making by generating sequential trajectories of states, actions, and observations. However, in complex, long-horizon tasks, these agents frequently suffer from compounding errors and struggle to recover from failures. Existing self-correction mechanisms rely on prompt-based reflection, which is inherently brittle, incurs heavy time and API costs due to iterative trial-and-error loops, and produces task-specific memory that may be hard to generalize to new scenarios. To address this, we propose Experience Memory Graph (EMG), a framework that reformulates agent failure recovery as a graph matching problem. At training time, we convert both failed exploration trajectories and successful expert trajectories into directed action decision graphs. By matching these graphs, we extract common subgraphs (successful workflows) and graph edit paths that explicitly indicate how to correct failures (e.g., which actions to add, delete, or relabel under a given observation), and store them in a memory graph with intra-task nodes and cross-task edges. At test time, EMG retrieves relevant insights and guides the agent in a single, loop-free execution. Experiments on ALFWorld and ScienceWorld show that EMG consistently outperforms state-of-the-art reflection baselines in success rate and average reward, while requiring no test-time trial-and-error.

AI.tldr: A new framework, Experience Memory Graph (EMG), helps LLM-based agents recover from failures in long-horizon tasks by learning generalized correction strategies from past trajectories via graph matching, achieving better performance without costly trial-and-error reflection at test time.
AI.motivation: LLM agents can act autonomously in sequential decision-making tasks but perform poorly on complex, long-horizon problems due to compounding errors and weak failure recovery. Existing self-correction via prompt-based reflection is brittle, slow, and expensive, and often does not generalize well beyond specific tasks. There is a need for a more structured, reusable way to capture and apply lessons from past failures and successes so that agents can correct themselves efficiently in new situations.
AI.method: The paper introduces Experience Memory Graph (EMG). During training, both failed exploration trajectories and successful expert trajectories are converted into directed action decision graphs. A graph matching process identifies: (1) common subgraphs representing successful workflows and (2) graph edit paths highlighting how to transform failure graphs into success graphs (actions to add, delete, or relabel in specific observations). These patterns are stored in a global memory graph that connects intra-task nodes and cross-task edges. At test time, instead of iterative reflection, the agent queries this memory graph to retrieve relevant subgraphs and correction patterns and then executes a single, guided trajectory without trial-and-error loops.
AI.result: On ALFWorld and ScienceWorld benchmarks, EMG achieves higher success rates and average rewards than state-of-the-art reflection-based baselines. It does so while avoiding test-time trial-and-error, thereby reducing time and API costs. The empirical results indicate that EMG’s graph-based memory of corrections generalizes across tasks more effectively than prompt-based reflection memories.
AI.conclusion: Representing agent experiences as structured graphs and learning corrections through graph matching provides a more robust and efficient mechanism for failure recovery than prompt-based reflection. EMG enables LLM agents to perform better on complex, long-horizon tasks with a single, loop-free execution, while also offering cross-task generalization of learned correction strategies.

title: DeltaMerge-LowRes: Composing Language and Task Deltas for Low-Resource Adaptation
summary: Adapting a multilingual encoder to a new language \emph{and} a new task with only a few hundred gold examples is a common low-resource NLP setting, yet the two axes are usually fused via an expensive language--task fine-tuning run. We ask whether they can instead be trained separately and recombined in weight space. \DeltaMergeLowRes{} learns a language delta $Δ_L$ from unlabeled monolingual text and a task delta $Δ_T$ from labeled English data, then composes them at inference under one of four rules: additive, activation-guided, sparsity-aware, and a novel \emph{cross-axis TIES}. The new rule adapts the TIES-Merging steps of trimming, sign election, and merging to the language and task axes rather than to two task axes. Holding $(Δ_L,Δ_T)$ fixed across rules on four task families and four African languages ($158$ evaluated cells, $10{,}000$-sample paired bootstrap per cell), we find: (i) cross-axis TIES wins summarisation on $3/4$ languages by $+4$ to $+7$ chrF (chrF $18.59$ vs.\ $13.80$ task-only); (ii) it improves QA F1 by $+2.32$ and EM by $+2.91$; and (iii) sparsity-aware merging cuts classification ECE by $36\%$ at parity macro-F1. The composition rule materially changes what the merged model preserves, suppresses, and calibrates. We release all JSON traces and a claim ledger.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ΔMergeLowRes, a method to separately learn language and task adaptations for multilingual encoders and then merge them in weight space using specialized composition rules, achieving strong gains in low-resource languages with minimal labeled data.
AI.motivation: Low-resource NLP often requires adapting models to both a new language and a new task, but standard practice fuses these through a single expensive fine-tuning run per language–task pair. This is inefficient and limits scalability when labeled data are scarce. The authors want to know whether language adaptation and task adaptation can instead be trained separately and then recombined later, enabling cheaper and more modular transfer to many low-resource languages.
AI.method: The authors introduce ΔMergeLowRes, which decomposes adaptation into two deltas in weight space: a language delta Δ_L learned from unlabeled monolingual text, and a task delta Δ_T learned from labeled English data. At inference time, they compose these deltas with the base multilingual encoder using four different weight-space merging rules: (1) simple additive merging, (2) activation-guided merging, (3) sparsity-aware merging, and (4) a new cross-axis TIES rule that adapts TIES-Merging (trimming, sign election, and merging) to operate across language and task axes instead of across tasks. They evaluate each rule while holding (Δ_L, Δ_T) fixed across conditions, on four task families and four African languages, using extensive paired bootstrap resampling for significance testing.
AI.result: Cross-axis TIES merging substantially improves summarization performance on 3 of 4 African languages, with chrF gains of +4 to +7 over a task-only baseline (e.g., chrF 18.59 vs. 13.80). For QA tasks, it raises F1 by +2.32 and exact match by +2.91. For classification, sparsity-aware merging reduces expected calibration error by 36% while maintaining the same macro-F1 performance. These results demonstrate that the choice of composition rule has a large impact on which aspects of language and task knowledge are preserved and how well-calibrated the final model is.
AI.conclusion: Separately learning language and task adaptations and then recombining them in weight space is feasible and effective in low-resource multilingual settings. The specific composition rule—especially the proposed cross-axis TIES and sparsity-aware variants—strongly influences performance, calibration, and what the merged model preserves or suppresses. ΔMergeLowRes offers a more modular and data-efficient pathway for bringing new languages and tasks into multilingual encoders, and the authors support their claims with extensive evaluations and public release of experimental traces and a claim ledger.

title: AIMO Interpretability Challenge
summary: We propose the AIMO Interpretability Challenge, a competition on distinguishing robust from spurious reasoning in frontier mathematical language models based on the models' internal mechanisms. The challenge is motivated by a central limitation of standard reasoning benchmarks: strong final-answer accuracy does not reveal whether a model relies on stable reasoning mechanisms or exploits brittle reasoning shortcuts. Building on AI Mathematical Olympiad (AIMO) problems and submissions, together with resources from the Fields Model Initiative, the competition will provide (1) newly-published olympiad-level math reasoning problems and their symbolic representations, allowing generation of novel functional variants, (2) access to frontier reasoning models, and (3) assessments of models' adversarial robustness on these problems. Participants will use these resources, along with our computing infrastructure support, to develop methods for identifying which models solve problems robustly. Our competition will also create a new, open robustness benchmark and baseline systems, aiming to provide a lasting foundation for standard benchmarking in mathematical reasoning and interpretability. Scientifically, the competition connects interpretability and generalization research around a central question in AI research: can we determine if, and to what extent, the decision-making of frontier AI models is generalizable and thus, reliable?

AI.tldr: They propose a competition to test whether math AI models use robust, generalizable reasoning or fragile shortcuts, by analyzing internal mechanisms on Olympiad-level problems.
AI.motivation: Existing reasoning benchmarks mostly look at whether models get the final answer right, which can be misleading because models might use brittle shortcuts rather than genuinely robust reasoning. There is a need for tools and standards to distinguish stable, generalizable reasoning from spurious correlations or hacks in frontier mathematical reasoning models.
AI.method: Design a competition, the AIMO Interpretability Challenge, built on AI Mathematical Olympiad (AIMO) problems plus Fields Model Initiative resources. They will provide new olympiad-level problems and symbolic formulations that enable generating functional variants, give access to frontier reasoning models, and evaluate adversarial robustness on these problems. Participants, using shared compute, must develop interpretability and analysis methods that identify which models are solving problems robustly based on their internal mechanisms rather than just outputs.
AI.result: The immediate result is the construction of a challenge framework: a dataset of olympiad-level math problems with symbolic structure, access protocols for frontier models, an adversarial robustness evaluation setup, and baseline systems for interpretability-based robustness identification. It yields a new open robustness benchmark centered on mathematical reasoning and model internals.
AI.conclusion: The competition will provide a standardized, open platform for assessing and comparing robustness and interpretability of frontier mathematical reasoning models. It aims to bridge interpretability and generalization research and to help answer whether, and to what extent, a model’s internal decision-making is genuinely generalizable and therefore reliable, laying groundwork for longer-term benchmarking in this area.

title: Constraint-Aware Counterfactual Editing for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
comment: 15 pages, 1 figure, and 5 tables. Accepted for presentation at the 2nd International Workshop on Informing ML with Knowledge Engineering for Hybrid Intelligent Systems (HHAI-KEML 2026), Brussels, Belgium
summary: Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) requires models to identify sentiment toward specific aspects rather than relying on the global polarity of a sentence. This makes counterfactual evaluation especially challenging: a valid counterfactual should flip the sentiment of one target aspect while preserving the sentiment of all non-target aspects, semantic meaning, fluency, and factual consistency. Existing counterfactual generation methods often focus on sentence-level label flipping and may produce edits that are fluent but aspect-invalid, semantically drifting, or contradictory. To address this limitation, we propose CAVE-ABSA, a Constraint-Aware Validated Editing framework for generating and validating aspect-level counterfactuals. CAVE-ABSA localizes the opinion span associated with the target aspect, performs controlled counterfactual rewriting, refines candidates through a repair module, and filters them using aspect-level verification, semantic similarity, AMR-guided structural preservation, edit minimality, fluency, and contradiction detection. The framework is designed to construct validated counterfactual ABSA datasets for robustness evaluation and data augmentation. By explicitly separating generation from validation, CAVE-ABSA provides a principled approach for producing meaningful aspect-local counterfactuals and for testing whether ABSA models truly rely on aspect-grounded sentiment reasoning.

AI.tldr: They propose CAVE-ABSA, a framework to generate and strictly validate aspect-level counterfactual sentences for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis, ensuring only the target aspect’s sentiment flips while everything else stays consistent.
AI.motivation: Existing counterfactual data generation mainly targets sentence-level sentiment flips and often fails for ABSA: changes may unintentionally alter non-target aspects, drift semantically, or introduce contradictions. ABSA needs fine-grained, aspect-local counterfactuals to evaluate robustness and improve training, but current methods don’t enforce aspect-level correctness and multiple linguistic constraints.
AI.method: CAVE-ABSA is a constraint-aware editing pipeline. It first localizes the opinion span tied to a chosen aspect, then performs controlled rewriting to flip that aspect’s sentiment. Generated candidates are passed through a repair module and then through a multi-constraint validator: aspect-level sentiment verification, semantic similarity checks, AMR-based structural preservation, edit minimality, fluency assessment, and contradiction detection. Generation and validation are explicitly separated so many candidates can be filtered down to only high-quality counterfactuals.
AI.result: The abstract states that CAVE-ABSA can construct validated counterfactual ABSA datasets suitable for robustness evaluation and data augmentation. While no numbers are given, the implication is that their framework yields higher-quality, aspect-correct, and linguistically sound counterfactuals than prior sentence-level editing methods.
AI.conclusion: CAVE-ABSA offers a principled, constraint-aware way to create aspect-local counterfactuals by decoupling generation from strict validation. This enables more meaningful testing of whether ABSA models actually use aspect-grounded sentiment reasoning and provides reliable counterfactual data for improving model robustness.

title: A Self-Evolving Agent for Longitudinal Personal Health Management
comment: 20 pages, 4 figures, 6 supplementary tables. Code: https://github.com/HC-Guo/HealthClaw
summary: Personal health management unfolds over repeated encounters, yet most health AI systems treat each request in isolation. We developed HealthClaw, an open-source agent architecture that updates support as a person's routines, preferences, measurements and risks change.
