It separates shared safety rules and medical knowledge from private longitudinal memory containing profile facts, reusable procedures and episodic traces. After each episode, induction determines what should update the profile, revise a procedure, remain episodic or be excluded. We evaluated HealthClaw with a synthetic year-long benchmark and nine 200-case biomedical tasks. Across 900 longitudinal support probes, answer accuracy increased from 0.2% with current-query prompting to 45.7% with HealthClaw, while prompt-side context exposure was 71.7% lower than with full-history prompting. In 100 privacy probes, HealthClaw produced higher privacy-aware answer quality and fewer unsafe disclosures than both baselines. Across the biomedical tasks, the mean absolute gain in the task-specific primary metric was 27.0 percentage points, and seven gains remained significant after false-discovery-rate correction. These offline benchmarks support governed, self-evolving memory for longitudinal personal health agents, although clinical effectiveness requires prospective evaluation. HealthClaw is publicly available at https://github.com/HC-Guo/HealthClaw.

AI.tldr: HealthClaw is an open-source agent architecture that maintains governed, self-updating long‑term memory for personal health management, greatly improving accuracy and privacy over standard prompting baselines in offline benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Most health AI systems respond to each user query independently and ignore the longitudinal nature of personal health management, where routines, risks, preferences and measurements evolve over time. This limits their ability to provide coherent, personalized, and safe support while also risking unnecessary exposure of historical data. The paper aims to design an AI architecture that can leverage long‑term context efficiently, keep safety and medical knowledge separate from personal data, and manage privacy‑aware use of a person’s health history.
AI.method: The authors propose HealthClaw, an agent architecture with two key separations: (1) a shared layer containing global safety rules and medical knowledge, and (2) a private, longitudinal memory that stores user‑specific profile facts, reusable procedures and episodic traces. After each interaction episode, an induction process decides what information should be promoted to stable profile data, incorporated into or modifying reusable procedures, left as episodic memory, or discarded. They evaluate the system using a synthetic year‑long benchmark for personal health management and nine biomedical tasks with 200 cases each, comparing HealthClaw against current‑query prompting and full‑history prompting baselines on accuracy, context length, and privacy behavior.
AI.result: On 900 longitudinal support probes, HealthClaw raised answer accuracy from 0.2% with current‑query prompting to 45.7%, while using 71.7% less prompt‑side context than full‑history prompting. In 100 privacy‑focused probes, HealthClaw generated higher‑quality, privacy‑aware answers and reduced unsafe disclosures relative to both baselines. Across nine biomedical tasks, HealthClaw improved the primary task metrics by an average of 27.0 percentage points, with seven of these gains remaining statistically significant after false‑discovery‑rate correction.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that governed, self‑evolving long‑term memory architectures like HealthClaw can substantially improve longitudinal personal health support in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and privacy management compared to conventional prompting approaches. However, the evidence is based on offline benchmarks; establishing clinical effectiveness and safety will require prospective, real‑world evaluations. HealthClaw is released as an open-source resource to support further research and development.

title: Can an Old Dog Be Taught New Tricks? Taking LLMs Beyond Sentence Level Translation
comment: Accepted for publication in HCI International 2026, Late Breaking Papers Proceedings, Springer LNCS
summary: Automatic translation systems, from CAT tools to MT, overwhelmingly treat translation as a sentence-by-sentence act. This paper asks whether LLMs can be moved beyond that paradigm through whole-document, corpus-informed translation. We present PAT (Pragmatic Auto-Translator), a RAG-based system that pairs user-configured specifications with context from a comparable corpus of authentic longform texts in U.S. English and Latin American Spanish, passing retrieved paragraph-, section-, and document-level examples to an LLM for whole-document generation. The goal is draft translation for professional verification: target texts reformulated to fit their Spanish-language context, where discourse organization, rhetorical style, and pragmatic norms differ meaningfully from English. We evaluated six automatic translations of essays on generative AI across three projects using a customized MQM typology, assessed by two trained evaluators working from U.S. English into LATAM and Mexican Spanish. Results show that a limited prompt produced no meaningful reformulation, and specifications and corpus-informed translations at times showed substantial reformulation, though not always to effect. We find that LLMs can be moved toward reformulation and away from the sentence-by-sentence paradigm, though more work is needed to improve the effectiveness of those reformulations. In this paper, we discuss considerations related to automatic translation system design, corpus construction, and translation quality evaluation methodology and results.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) can move beyond sentence-by-sentence translation by using a RAG-based system, PAT, that leverages corpus-level context and user specifications to generate whole-document, pragmatically appropriate translations from U.S. English into Latin American Spanish.
AI.motivation: Most current translation tools, from CAT to MT, treat translation as a sentence-by-sentence process, which limits their ability to produce pragmatically appropriate, discourse-aware target texts, especially when source and target languages differ in rhetorical style and norms. The authors want to explore whether LLMs, combined with corpus context and user specifications, can generate translations that better fit the target language’s discourse organization and pragmatic conventions, particularly for English to Latin American Spanish.
AI.method: They design PAT (Pragmatic Auto-Translator), a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system that: (1) lets users specify translation requirements (e.g., variety of Spanish, rhetorical style); (2) retrieves paragraph-, section-, and document-level examples from a comparable corpus of longform texts in U.S. English and Latin American Spanish; and (3) feeds these, along with the source document, to an LLM to generate whole-document translations. They evaluate six automatic translations of essays about generative AI across three projects, using a customized MQM-based error typology and two trained evaluators translating into LATAM and Mexican Spanish.
AI.result: A basic, minimally guided prompt led the LLM to produce largely literal, sentence-by-sentence translations with little meaningful reformulation. When user specifications and corpus-informed context were added, the LLM produced translations that sometimes showed substantial reformulation at discourse and pragmatic levels, but these changes were not consistently beneficial. The results indicate that corpus-guided prompting can nudge LLMs away from sentence-level translation, but the quality and usefulness of reformulations are uneven.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can be steered toward whole-document, pragmatically informed reformulation instead of purely sentence-by-sentence translation when supported by RAG and detailed specifications, but the approach is not yet reliably effective. The authors highlight implications for designing automatic translation systems that leverage comparable corpora, for building suitable bilingual corpora for such tasks, and for refining translation quality evaluation methods to capture discourse-level and pragmatic phenomena.

title: Do Agent Optimizers Compound? A Continual-Learning Evaluation on Terminal-Bench 2.0
summary: Most reported gains from agent-optimization methods are one-shot: an agent is optimized against a fixed benchmark and the resulting improvement is reported as if it were a stable property of the method. This does not test the setting that matters for deployed agents, where optimization is applied recursively as new failures and new tasks appear over time. The central question this raises is whether optimizer-driven gains compound: after an agent has been optimized once, can it be optimized again on newly arrived tasks without eroding the gains the first round produced? We study this question with a two-phase continual-learning evaluation built from hard tasks in Terminal-Bench 2.0, comparing three approaches to agent-harness optimization (GEPA, Meta Harness, and RELAI's Verifiable Continual Learning, RELAI-VCL) under identical optimization budgets. All three methods improve over the baseline agent in the conventional, static, single-phase setting. However, once new tasks are introduced, the methods diverge sharply: GEPA's optimized agent transfers below the unoptimized baseline, Meta Harness transfers well but fails to improve further once given a second optimization budget, and RELAI-VCL is the only method that both transfers positively to unseen tasks and continues improving after those tasks are folded into the optimization objective, reaching the highest pass rate at every evaluated stage and the highest lifelong average pass rate overall (76.4% vs. 66.0% for GEPA, 64.6% for Meta Harness, and 58.7% for the baseline). Our key observation was that optimization gains compounded only when regression control was built into the optimization loop, providing an inductive bias against shortcut solutions that fail to generalize.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether performance gains from agent-optimization methods compound over time in realistic continual-learning settings, finding that only an approach with explicit regression control (RELAI-VCL) both generalizes to new tasks and continues improving under repeated optimization.
AI.motivation: Most optimization methods for AI agents are evaluated in a static, one-shot benchmark setting, which does not reflect real deployments where agents face new tasks and failures over time and are repeatedly re-optimized. The authors want to know whether the performance gains from these optimizations persist and accumulate, or if they regress or saturate when new tasks appear and further optimization is applied. This addresses the practical question of how to design optimization procedures that lead to robust, compounding improvements rather than brittle, benchmark-specific overfitting.
AI.method: The authors design a two-phase continual-learning evaluation based on hard tasks from Terminal-Bench 2.0. In phase one, several agent-optimization methods—GEPA, Meta Harness, and RELAI’s Verifiable Continual Learning (RELAI-VCL)—are each given the same optimization budget to improve a baseline agent on an initial task set. In phase two, new tasks are introduced to assess transfer and continual learning: the previously optimized agents are evaluated on these new tasks and then given an additional, equal optimization budget that now includes the new tasks in the objective. The methods are compared on transfer performance to unseen tasks, their ability to further improve after the second optimization round, and overall lifelong average pass rate.
AI.result: In the standard one-shot (single-phase, static) benchmark setting, all three optimization methods—GEPA, Meta Harness, and RELAI-VCL—improve over the unoptimized baseline agent. However, their behavior diverges in the continual-learning setting. GEPA’s optimized agent actually performs worse than the original baseline when transferred to new tasks, indicating negative transfer and overfitting to the initial tasks. Meta Harness transfers well to unseen tasks but shows no additional improvement when given a second optimization budget that includes the new tasks, suggesting a plateau in compounding gains. RELAI-VCL both transfers positively to new tasks and continues to improve after those tasks are incorporated into the optimization objective. It achieves the highest performance at every stage and the best lifelong average pass rate: 76.4% compared to 66.0% for GEPA, 64.6% for Meta Harness, and 58.7% for the baseline agent.
AI.conclusion: Performance gains from agent-optimization methods do not automatically compound in realistic continual-learning scenarios. Some methods overfit to initial tasks and regress on new ones (GEPA), while others transfer but do not further improve under additional optimization (Meta Harness). Compounding gains were observed only in RELAI-VCL, which integrates regression control into the optimization loop to prevent shortcut, non-generalizing solutions. The findings suggest that effective, long-term agent optimization requires mechanisms that explicitly guard against regressions and encourage robust generalization, rather than relying solely on static benchmark improvements.

title: Hindcast: Replaying Prediction Markets to Evaluate LLM Forecasters
summary: Forecasters are evaluated by backtesting, which replays resolved questions and grades the probability the system would have assigned before the outcome was known. For LLMs, two channels leak the answer into this test. A model that retrieves can surface reports written after the event, turning forecasting into a lookup, and each new model is trained on data closer to the event, so a question that lay in the future for last year's models sits inside this year's training data. Either way, the test grades recall while claiming to grade foresight. We introduce Hindcast, which closes both leaks by grading a model as if it stood at a chosen past date $t_0$, before the outcome existed in either channel. Hindcast replays resolved Polymarket prediction markets against a frozen snapshot of public Reddit, lets the model read only posts written before $t_0$, and scores each forecast against both what happened and the market's own price at $t_0$, itself a human forecast made from the same past information. Because the cutoff is set per market and the snapshot never changes, the evaluation re-runs on new markets as models improve, without going stale. Once the leak is closed, retrieval still helps most models, but only where Reddit discussed the event beforehand. Where the archive carried only speculation, retrieval hurts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hindcast, an evaluation framework that prevents information leakage when backtesting LLM forecasters by simulating what the model could have known at a specific past time.
AI.motivation: Standard backtesting of forecasting systems, especially LLMs, is compromised because models can access information produced after the event via retrieval, or have been trained on data closer to the event, effectively turning forecasting into answering questions with hindsight. This means current evaluations often measure how well models recall outcomes rather than how well they predict them from prior information. The authors want an evaluation method that truly measures foresight.
AI.method: The authors propose Hindcast, which evaluates LLM forecasts as if the model were situated at a chosen past date t0, before the outcome or any post-event information existed. They use resolved Polymarket prediction markets and a frozen snapshot of public Reddit. For each market, they set a market-specific cutoff date t0, restrict retrieval to Reddit posts written before t0, and then score model forecasts on two axes: (1) calibration with respect to the actual outcome, and (2) accuracy relative to the Polymarket price at t0, which reflects human forecasts based on the same information. The Reddit snapshot is fixed, and t0 is defined per market, so new models can be evaluated on the same historical information without the benchmark going stale.
AI.result: They find that when information leakage is removed, retrieval still improves forecasting performance for most models, but only for events that Reddit had concretely discussed before t0. In cases where Reddit discussion was sparse or purely speculative, retrieval degrades performance, presumably by exposing models to low-quality or noisy signals. The framework remains reusable over time because it is tied to a fixed historical data snapshot and market-specific cutoffs rather than the models' evolving training data.
AI.conclusion: Hindcast offers a leakage-resistant way to evaluate LLM-based forecasting by faithfully simulating past information states. It reveals that retrieval helps only when relevant, substantive pre-event discussion exists, and can hurt when the available text is speculative. Because the benchmark is defined on fixed past data and per-market cutoffs, it can be used to compare future models without becoming contaminated by hindsight information, making it a more reliable standard for measuring genuine foresight.

title: AI-accelerated End-to-End Framework for Rapid Professional Upskilling
summary: By 2030, 59 of every 100 workers will need reskilling or upskilling, yet the average time to close an enterprise skills gap grew from roughly 3 days in 2014 to 36 days in 2018. Most current frameworks accelerate single stages of upskilling programs and generally lack industry validation. We present an end-to-end framework that applies AI acceleration across five stages of knowledge acquisition, content development, content review and verification, teaching, and assessment development; with a strong focus on both production and learning efficiency. Three strong external signals validates the framework: the US National Association of State Boards of Accountancy reviewed and approved an upskilling program built on the framework for continuing-professional-education credits; 3 learners followed the program and passed the NVIDIA Certified Professional in Agentic AI exam in a significantly short amount of time, with 14 more in progress; the program's knowledge base supports complex downstream analysis such as the production of a robust 1,267 risk item dataset for managing multi-agent AI system risks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an AI-accelerated, end-to-end upskilling framework that significantly speeds up professional training across five stages, and shows early external validation through accreditation, exam pass rates, and use in downstream risk analysis.
AI.motivation: Reskilling needs are exploding—over half the workforce will need new skills by 2030—yet the time required to close enterprise skills gaps is increasing dramatically. Existing solutions usually speed up only isolated parts of the learning pipeline and lack strong validation from industry and professional bodies. There is a need for a comprehensive, proven framework that uses AI to improve both the creation and delivery of training and the learning effectiveness itself.
AI.method: The authors design an end-to-end framework that uses AI tools to accelerate five key stages of upskilling: (1) knowledge acquisition, (2) content development, (3) content review and verification, (4) teaching/delivery, and (5) assessment development. The framework emphasizes both production efficiency (faster development and updating of learning materials) and learning efficiency (faster, effective learner progress). They then instantiate the framework in a concrete upskilling program focused on agentic AI.
AI.result: An upskilling program built with this framework received formal approval for continuing professional education credits from the US National Association of State Boards of Accountancy. In early trials, 3 learners who followed the program passed the NVIDIA Certified Professional in Agentic AI exam in notably short time, with 14 additional learners still in progress. The same program’s knowledge base was successfully used to generate a large, 1,267-item risk dataset for managing multi-agent AI system risks, demonstrating the framework’s ability to support complex downstream analytical tasks.
AI.conclusion: The AI-accelerated end-to-end upskilling framework can substantially improve both the speed and quality of professional training, and its initial deployment has gained external accreditation, produced rapid learner success, and enabled sophisticated risk analysis artifacts. These signals suggest the framework is a promising approach to closing widening enterprise skills gaps, though broader deployment and larger-scale studies would be needed to fully confirm its effectiveness.

title: Earthquaker-AI: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework with Rubric-Based Assessment for Primary School Earthquake Education
summary: This paper presents Earthquaker-AI, a hybrid educational framework building upon a previously implemented educational robotics project by integrating a conversational AI assistant based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation. It aims to enhance earthquake preparedness and conscious action among primary-school students. The system extends the award-winning STEM project Earthquaker moving from mechanical simulation with Lego WeDo2 to cognitive and metacognitive processing. The robotics component uses Lego WeDo2 automation to simulate seismic response, letting students interact with sensors and actuators as tangible representations of protective actions. The assistant operates as a guided learning mechanism aligning student responses with safety guidelines, while providing rubric-based verbal feedback that supports self-regulated learning and calmness under emergency conditions. Earthquaker-AI follows a progressive learning trajectory aligned with cognitive development. In early grades, the focus is on basic recognition of safety actions through multiple-choice questions, assessed via a two-dimensional rubric. In middle grades, students identify correct action sequences through multiple-choice questions, evaluated via a three-axis rubric. In upper grades, the approach shifts to verbal production, requiring short written responses assessed via a four-dimensional rubric that includes clarity of expression. The dialogic module uses RAG to match student queries semantically with official guidelines, generating safe, accurate responses. Experimental evaluation shows high groundedness and accuracy, with a low hallucination rate. Overall, Earthquaker-AI combines hands-on engagement, information processing, and reflective practice. Combining robotics, rubrics, and AI promotes technological literacy, self-regulation, and responsible use of digital systems, contributing to early crisis-management skills.

AI.tldr: Earthquaker-AI is a hybrid educational system that combines Lego-based robotics with a conversational RAG-based AI assistant to teach primary students earthquake safety through progressively more complex, rubric-assessed activities.
AI.motivation: To improve earthquake preparedness and calm, safety-aligned behaviour among primary-school students by moving beyond mechanical simulations toward cognitive and metacognitive learning supported by AI, and to foster technological literacy and responsible digital use from an early age.
AI.method: Extend an existing Lego WeDo2 robotics project (Earthquaker) with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation conversational assistant and rubric-based assessment along a developmental trajectory: younger students answer basic multiple-choice questions on safety actions; middle grades choose correct action sequences; upper grades provide short written responses evaluated on four rubric dimensions including clarity. The RAG module semantically matches student inputs to official guidelines and generates grounded, safety-compliant feedback, while the robotics setup physically simulates seismic responses via sensors and actuators.
AI.result: The experimental evaluation of the dialogic RAG module shows high groundedness and accuracy in responses, with a low hallucination rate, indicating reliable alignment with official safety guidelines. The integrated framework effectively supports hands-on interaction, information processing, and reflective practice for students.
AI.conclusion: Earthquaker-AI successfully fuses robotics, rubric-guided assessment, and RAG-based conversational AI into a progressive learning framework that enhances earthquake preparedness, self-regulated learning, technological literacy, and responsible digital behaviour in primary-school students, contributing to early crisis-management competencies.

title: Deep Interaction: An Efficient Human-AI Interaction Method for Large Reasoning Models
summary: The emergence of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has significantly enhanced the ability of large language models (LLMs) to tackle complex, multi-step tasks. However, when errors occur, current interaction approaches typically involve re-generating another response that may make mistakes again, or users laboriously flag the faulty step in follow-up turns that may get responses <You are right, I made a mistake here> followed by similar errors recurring. To address this issue, we propose an efficient human intervention mechanism for precisely correcting reasoning errors in LLMs, termed Deep Interaction. Our approach enables direct editing of the original response, allowing erroneous parts to be corrected while preserving accurate reasoning steps. We refine the edited CoT into a distilled prompt, which then steers the LLM along the corrected reasoning path. Experimental results show that our method achieves over a 25% improvement in correction success rate and reduces token usage by approximately 40% on STEM tasks reasoning compared to baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a human-in-the-loop method, Deep Interaction, to precisely fix errors in LLM chain-of-thought reasoning by editing the original reasoning steps and then refining them into a guiding prompt, improving correction success and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-Thought reasoning helps LLMs solve complex tasks, but when they make a mistake, current ways of correcting them are inefficient: simply re-generating answers can repeat errors, and asking users to point out faulty steps often leads to superficial acknowledgments without real correction. The authors want a more precise, efficient way to correct specific reasoning errors while keeping the correct parts intact.
AI.method: They introduce Deep Interaction, a mechanism where users or an external process directly edit the model’s initial chain-of-thought, correcting only the erroneous segments while preserving correct steps. The system then converts this edited chain-of-thought into a distilled prompt that encapsulates the corrected reasoning and feeds it back to the LLM so that subsequent generations follow the corrected reasoning path.
AI.result: On STEM reasoning tasks, Deep Interaction yields over 25% higher success rate in correcting previous reasoning errors compared to baselines, and cuts token usage by about 40%, showing both higher effectiveness and better efficiency in human-in-the-loop correction.
AI.conclusion: Directly editing and distilling prior chains-of-thought into guiding prompts is an effective way to correct LLM reasoning errors. Deep Interaction enables accurate, token-efficient interventions that outperform simple re-generation or naive step-flagging approaches for complex reasoning tasks.
